Matched fieldsabstract

69. Trade Unions and States

British Journal of Industrial Relations 2008-11-04 Available
JournalBritish Journal of Industrial Relations
Publication date2008-11-04
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-8543.2008.00701.x
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Colin CrouchUniversity of Warwick; University of Warwick Business School
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Trade Unions and the State: The Construction of Industrial Relations Institutions in Britain, 1890–2000 by Howell, Chris . Princeton University Press , Princeton, NJ , 2005 , xi + 243 pp., ISBN 0 691 12106 0 , £26.95 That the history of British trade unionism in the twentieth century was a story of hubris would be widely accepted; but in this detailed, highly scholarly historical account of the case, Chris Howell proposes an original and far from widely accepted key component of that hubris. While the unions, their opponents and most academic observers have considered that the classic British system was a voluntarist one, Howell argues that union strength was always dependent on support from the state. When, by the end of the 1970s, the state withdrew that support from both unions and the institutions of collective bargaining, the apparently autonomous and resilient edifice of union power collapsed easily. Howell's account is thoroughly convincing and signals a need to reconsider much more in the history of British industrial relations, and indeed of British society. Is this a country in which civil society is so strong that it can procure its collective goods through the autonomous actions of its citizens without much action by government? Or is it one that just tries to get by with a minimum of collective goods? The author offers the following periodization of his terrain: 1890–1940, the construction of the collective laissez faire system; 1940–1979, dissension and the decentralization of industrial relations; and 1979–1997, decollectivization. The trap of the hubris is set during the second, essentially unstable period, being finally sprung early in the third. During the first period, the basic framework was established of a system that brought a degree of order and industrial relations stability to an economy that was highly fragmented, with disorganized, medium-sized firms and a highly divided workforce. It was during this period that governments more or less consistently perceived the need for some institution construction to avoid chaos and disorder. British capital, dominated as it was by the financial sector on one side and a fragmented set of manufacturing industries on another, was unable to solve its own collective action problem of establishing an order from which it would itself benefit, so government acted. Central to Howell's highly convincing account is the idea of the state simultaneously supporting and constraining the power of employers by nudging and prodding them towards collective industrial relations arrangements. Employers were being reluctantly constitutionalized in their relations with the collectivities of their employees, just as the state itself had been constitutionalized in its relations with its collective public. Because the liberal British state proceeded by nudging and prodding rather than by the elaboration of formal structures of rights and duties, unions came to believe that they had brought employers to the construction of these institutions by their own strength. They failed to notice that they gradually became dependent on government encouragement of employers' associations, on whom the unions relied in turn more or less to recruit their members for them and enforce agreements. And they came to prefer the acquisition of immunities from the free trade logic of common law to the gaining of positive collective legal rights of labour. The theoretical position that underlies Howell's account of institution creation, stability and change is that of the French régulationistes, with change being driven primarily by shifts in the forms of capitalist accumulation, although — like most contemporary régulationistes— his account is considerably more nuanced than that school's original division of pre-Fordism, Fordism and post-Fordism. Complex changes are perceived as sectors and firm types rise and fall, but towards the end of the 1890–1940 period, the new one of mass-production, primarily domestically oriented, large firms of Fordism had become evident. These corporations were better able to achieve an industrial order within themselves than the earlier generation of craft-based or smaller-scale firms, and had less need of one that was imposed externally by the machinery of state encouragement, employers' associations, unions, industry-wide collective bargaining, and collective labour law. The second period, after 1940, was therefore one in which the earlier edifice was gradually being undermined. At another, more political, level, however, that edifice was reaching the acme of its strength. The decade of the 1940s, characterized by mass mobilization for war and then by Labour government, saw a major ramification of collectivism in industrial relations. Howell's excellent account thus enables us to make sense of the labour struggles of the 1950s, 1960s and 1970s in ways that go considerably beyond the existing literature, extensive as that is. Government and union leaderships remained wedded to the path dependence of the earlier period: collective bargaining and the other industrial relations institutions were still definitely ‘good things’, even if unruly elements kept stimulating a need for reforms; the purpose of the reforms, including the Industrial Relations Act 1971, was still to make the model work better. On the other side, an unconscious alliance of managers seeking a firm-level model and shop stewards seeking to exploit the power of organized labour made possible by government commitment to full employment was undermining the premises of collective laissez-faire. Eventually, government was left in the hopeless position of seeking simultaneously to centralize wage-setting and decentralize other aspects of industrial relations. But both sets of actors on the labour side, central leaderships and shop stewards, continued to believe that it was organized labour's own strength that guaranteed its position in British society, that it owed little to government for its survival, and that government would have to accept its enduring presence. Given Howell's thesis that state support was always central to union strength, all that was really required to undermine that strength was a departure from the assumptions of the previous 90 years by the political class. This, of course, came with the election in 1979 of a Conservative government fully converted to neoliberal economic ideas and a leading member of the array of political and economic forces that was set to undermine most of the assumptions of the postwar political economy throughout the industrial world. Once presumptions in favour of collective action and the maintenance of full employment were dropped, the firm-level model could become one in which managers confronted, not shop stewards, but individual employees with no capacity for joint action. End of contradiction. The dramatic act that laid bare the hubris was the miners' strike of 1984–1985, conducted by a union believing completely in the myth of the autonomy of union strength. Howell possibly underestimates the role of pure political conjuncture in producing this particular dénouement. The chance occurrence of the Falklands War and the fact that the Labour Party chose to split itself in two, both occurring in the early 1980s, together gave the Conservatives an insulation from what might have otherwise been the political consequences of the abandonment of full employment policies. In that case, neoliberalism might have had to advance through the compromises with organized labour that have characterized its path in most of the rest of western Europe, leading to a restructuring of the collective system rather than its collapse. Howell would claim that the illusory nature of the British collective laissez faire model meant that the collapse was inevitable, and that sooner or later, another contingency would have occurred. This is perhaps the case, but when one considers how European systems as diverse and as potentially unfavourable to organized labour as the Irish, the French and the Spanish have all mutated into new compromises between neoliberalism and various forms of collectivism, one wonders. Howell ends his third period at 1997 and then considers in a separate chapter the changes consequent on the election of a Labour government in that year. At the time he was writing, he had only a few years of that government to write about, and he can be forgiven for not feeling able to get any kind of perspective on it. However, his historian's instincts should have warned him that a periodization that has made no concessions to events as momentous as the election of the Liberal government of 1906, the First World War, the General Strike, or the interwar depression, is unlikely to need to take much notice of a change of government between what are today two essentially neoliberal parties. In fact, his account of the post-1997 years makes it clear that, once the passing of time has afforded him better perspective, he is likely to see a continuation of the trajectory of 1979–1997: Labour has continued primarily with a model of individualized work relations, changing the emphasis a little by conceding more individual rights to employees. The more recent study of the period by Davies and Freedland (Towards a Flexible Labour Market, Oxford University Press, 2007), which has the advantage of surveying a longer post-1997 period and of concentrating on an analysis of labour law, corroborates Howell's account, and draws a further conclusion: with the exception of measures required by European Union directives, most of Labour's extensions of labour rights are designed to encourage labour force participation by groups like single mothers and immigrants who, without certain rights, might not work. British labour rights have therefore moved from being collective immunities from common law offences to being individual encouragements to work. This kind of right belongs to a discourse concerning the balance between rights and responsibilities: one acquires a right because one offers something of value. This is often opposed to a concept of rights as part and parcel of citizenship: under a universal citizenship regime, one does not have to work in order to have the right to a fair trial or to vote. This is, however, a legalistic view of citizenship. In reality, various social groups have won rights when they had something to offer that the state needed. This dates back to the original obligation on Athenian citizens to offer military service. While this can appear to undermine rights, as they become conditional, it can also be seen as strengthening them. If a right is given through benevolence or adherence to abstract principle on the part of the state, there remains doubt as to what would happen if that benevolence of adherence to principle were to slip. Such rights can be lost. Indeed, it can be said that Howell's book is a demonstration of what happens when groups become dependent on governments for their rights. In his story there is added piquancy in that the unions believed that they had won their rights, that they had offered something in exchange for them, and that they would therefore be able to reclaim what they had offered as a deterrent against withdrawal of the rights. What unions essentially have to ‘offer’ in this sense is (negatively) restraint in their capacity to disrupt society and (positively) collaboration in the pursuit of shared national economic goals. This is rather similar to the deals that medieval nobilities offered to monarchs as the latter tried to fashion systems of social peace. But it would seem, from Howell's account, that the unions' offer had over the decades become too dependent on the state's definition of its terms. They had become more like the Russian aristocracy of the nineteenth century, whose originally autonomous rights and powers increasingly became translated into a table of ranks maintained and controlled by the tsars. There is therefore a major difference between an exchange of rights for obligations when both parties maintain some autonomy over the terms of the deal, and one in which one party administers the whole. Mistaking the latter for the former is the British union weakness that Howell identifies. Different again is the contrast that Davies and Freedland identify between those collective rights and those currently given to individuals in exchange for their commitment to participate in the labour force. The exchange here is unpolitical, or rather is political on one side only. Government continues to act highly politically and strategically in framing its labour policies, but individuals in an increasingly decollectivized labour market cannot respond in the same way. They cannot offer counterplans, or try to amend the terms of the rights/obligations exchange. They are more like customers than citizens; and customers do not have rights, only products. How durable is this new model of industrial relations likely to be? Howell concludes his book by saying: . . . the third system of industrial relations has achieved the kind of political consensus enjoyed by the first system but which eluded the second, and there is a strong case for seeing this as a long-lasting, stable set of industrial relations institutions (p. 193). However, in line with Howell's régulationiste approach, one must now ask how durable is the accumulation regime on which that political consensus and industrial relations regime have been based. Also, while there have been important historical specificities in the British case, for the discovery of which Howell's historical method is ideally suited, the British case is also one of a large number of societies which have gone through certain similar experiences. Throughout the advanced capitalist world, the years from the 1940s to the 1970s constituted a period defined by growing mass prosperity and a growing size and strength of the manual working class, within which trade unions were rooted. This differed from the years of depression and repression which had preceded it, and from the neoliberal period which started, with different timings and nuances, some time from the late 1970s onwards. The so-called Fordist period was rooted in manufacturing (particularly mass production and within that particularly motor vehicles), and therefore usually associated with a mass working class, which produced organizations and had to be propitiated if it were not to present challenges to the social order. Keynesian demand management operated similarly to Fordism in making possible a mass supply of goods and services, which supply itself stimulated demand. This in turn created employment, which further stimulated demand. Keynesianism also made possible the provision of social citizenship rights, as the provision of public services and rights-based transfer payments were the basis of the large sums of government spending that demand management required. Together, therefore, the production model and demand management model served the purpose of class propitiation, from which process labour also acquired rights. For the first time in history the economic and political advance of the mass of the population was both possible and the foundation of the prosperity and stability of the whole of society. In a real sense, Marx's idea of the working class as the class that embodied the future of the whole society was realized, albeit in the form of a class compromise and not as Marx had envisaged it. It was also a far more temporary model than Marx anticipated, although it did follow his Hegelian assumptions (which he had thought would cease with the working class) about how history changes by successive periods carrying within themselves the of their own as Howell's history of his period those of with its of the Keynesian state, had an and it a different set of leading class This to be at the when the of manufacturing employment had and the class of industrial society was The neoliberal period has been rooted in services, particularly financial the important exception of public the of the services sectors not to the citizens of they demand goods and services from government and market but the they have do not need to to it, and not to to make collective In some this is because their terms and are established on an individual in labour market they the consequences for their employment of any collective action. for the social order to only from from the challenges of and the of While the neoliberal economy is one in which the purpose of government is to rather than support in the Keynesian it is even more dependent on mass than the Keynesian This is by two neoliberalism is associated with and with a major of There is therefore a growing supply of goods from with labour and of the of British other no longer on the work of other demand is by if is often the case in the are not and is can to maintain their to increasingly in it has been possible for to their and other as In like the and British into acquisition far than could be to by in and Because are in their are not considered to be and can while neoliberal is on and While the goods with their increasingly from they can also to services, which are less likely to be further stimulating the from manufacturing to services employment within the advanced world. 70. Steering From Ministers and Departments

Public Management Review 2008-12-19 Available
JournalPublic Management Review
Publication date2008-12-19
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/14719030802493569
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Jan Rommel[email removed]
Johan ChristiaensRussian State Agrarian Correspondence University; [email removed]
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Abstract This article aims at contributing to the study of autonomous agencies by focusing on how they cope with steering from ministers and departments. Drawing from the trust literature, we look at how agencies co-operate with ministers and departments, in order to increase autonomy. A model is built to cluster several trust-building mechanisms and to link these to autonomy. We then apply this to a limited set of Flemish agencies. We conclude that agencies develop strategies to increase trust with the trustor. These strategies may be targeted either at the minister as a trustor or at the department as a competing trustee.

KeywordsSocial Capital and Networks; Public Policy and Administration Research; Nonprofit Sector and Volunteering; Flemish; Autonomy; Set (abstract data type); Order (exchange); Council of Ministers; Public relations; Public administration; Steering committee; Political science; Business; Computer science; Law; Engineering management; Engineering; Finance; Archaeology; Programming language; European union; Economic policy; History

71. The Moderating Role of Autonomy and Control on the Benefits of Written Emotion Expression

Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin 2008-12-29 Available
JournalPersonality and Social Psychology Bulletin
Publication date2008-12-29
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1177/0146167208328165
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Netta WeinsteinUniversity of Rochester; University of Rochester,; Department of Clinical and Social Sciences in Psychology, University of Rochester, Rochester, NY 14627, USA. [email removed]
Holley S. HodginsSkidmore College; Skidmore College;
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Two studies examined the hypothesis that relative to control motivation, autonomy motivation is associated with effective written expression and regulation, leading to positive emotional, physical, and cognitive outcomes over time. Participants viewed a Hiroshima-Nagasaki documentary in each of two sessions. Study 1 showed that dispositionally autonomous participants, particularly those who expressed, had positive well-being, energy, and memory after the second viewing. Study 2 explored benefits of situational motivation by priming autonomy and control. Results showed that dispositionally controlled individuals received the same benefits as autonomous individuals only when primed with autonomy and encouraged to express. Coding of writing content revealed that the benefits of autonomy were mediated by nondefensive and effective emotional processing, as reflected in greater use of self-referencing and cognitive mechanism words and lower use of concrete words. Results support the expectation that autonomy relates to effective expression and emotion regulation, leading to positive functioning over time.

KeywordsMental Health via Writing; Creativity in Education and Neuroscience; Impact of Technology on Adolescents; Psychology; Autonomy; Expression (computer science); Social psychology; Cognition; Situational ethics; Cognitive reappraisal; Priming (agriculture); Emotional regulation; Control (management); Emotional expression; Developmental psychology; Mechanism (biology); Biology; Political science; Neuroscience; Programming language; Philosophy; Epistemology; Germination; Botany; Economics; Computer science; Law; Management
Matched conceptsautonomous
Matched fieldsabstract

72. `We're not banks': Exploring self-discipline, subjectivity and co-operative work

Human Relations 2009-01-01 Available
JournalHuman Relations
Publication date2009-01-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1177/0018726708099516
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Anita ManganUniversity College Dublin; Centre for Innovation, Technology and Organization (CITO) in University College Dublin,; Department of Management
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

In studies of the workplace, the enterprising employee is theorized as autonomous, self-actualizing and calculating; self-discipline is linked to self-interest. This article explores the question of self-discipline by drawing on empirical material from a longitudinal study of the Irish credit union movement. As financial co-operatives, credit unions have a tradition of helping local communities under the aegis of mutuality and co-operative credit. The article theorizes two interrelated, yet antagonistic discourses within the credit union movement; an older community service discourse and an emerging enterprise discourse. In tracing the question of what it means to be a credit union volunteer, the article explores some of the tensions and contradictions between the two discourses, as experienced by volunteers who struggle with the question of how to balance the movement's traditional self-help ethos with the growing pressures to become more entrepreneurial.

KeywordsLabor Movements and Unions; Digital Economy and Work Transformation; Management and Organizational Studies; Ethos; Subjectivity; Irish; Sociology; Credit union; Public relations; Trade union; Political economy; Political science; Economics; Law; Epistemology; Labour economics; Finance; Linguistics; Philosophy

Matched fieldsabstract

73. Commitment insurance: Compensating for the autonomy costs of interdependence in close relationships.

Journal of Personality and Social Psychology 2009-01-01 Available
JournalJournal of Personality and Social Psychology
Publication date2009-01-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1037/a0014562
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Susan MurrayUniversity at Buffalo, State University of New York; Department of Psychology, University at Buffalo, The State University of New York, Buffalo, NY 14260-4110, USA. [email removed]; Department of Psychology, University at Buffalo, The State University of New York, Buffalo, NY 14260-4110, USA
John G. HolmesUniversity of Waterloo; Department of Psychology,University of Waterloo, Waterloo, ON,Canada
Maya AloniUniversity at Buffalo, State University of New York; Department of Psychology, University at Buffalo, the State University of New York, Buffalo, NY, US
Rebecca T. PinkusUniversity at Buffalo, State University of New York; Department of Psychology, University at Buffalo, the State University of New York, Buffalo, NY, US
Jaye L. DerrickUniversity at Buffalo, State University of New York; Department of Psychology, University at Buffalo, the State University of New York, Buffalo, NY, US
Sadie LederUniversity at Buffalo, State University of New York; Department of Psychology, University at Buffalo, the State University of New York, Buffalo, NY, US
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

A model of the commitment-insurance system is proposed to examine how low and high self-esteem people cope with the costs interdependence imposes on autonomous goal pursuits. In this system, autonomy costs automatically activate compensatory cognitive processes that attach greater value to the partner. Greater partner valuing compels greater responsiveness to the partner's needs. Two experiments and a daily diary study of newlyweds supported the model. Autonomy costs automatically activate more positive implicit evaluations of the partner. On explicit measures of positive illusions, high self-esteem people continue to compensate for costs. However, cost-primed low self-esteem people correct and override their positive implicit sentiments when they have the opportunity to do so. Such corrections put the marriages of low self-esteem people at risk: Failing to compensate for costs predicted declines in satisfaction over a 1-year period.

KeywordsAttachment and Relationship Dynamics; Evolutionary Psychology and Human Behavior; Cultural Differences and Values; Autonomy; Psychology; Social psychology; Illusion; Self-esteem; Value (mathematics); Opportunity cost; Economics; Microeconomics; Cognitive psychology; Law; Machine learning; Computer science; Political science

74. Transferability of the venture capital model to the corporate context: Implications for the performance of corporate venture units

Strategic Entrepreneurship Journal 2009-03-01 Available
JournalStrategic Entrepreneurship Journal
Publication date2009-03-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1002/sej.54
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Susan A. HillLondon School of Economics and Political Science; London School of Economics and Political Science, London, U.K; London School of Economics and Political Science London U.K
Markku MaulaHelsinki University of Technology, Institute of Strategy and International Business, Helsinki, Finland
Julian BirkinshawLondon Business School; London Business School, London, U.K
Gordon MurrayUniversity of Exeter; University of Exeter, School of Business and Economics, Exeter, U.K
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Abstract While recent literature has advocated the transfer of venture capital (VC) practices and structures to corporate venture (CV) units, the wisdom of this advice has not been subject to rigorous empirical scrutiny. We test the implications of the VC model for CV unit performance using a longitudinal database combining survey, archival, and survival data on 95 CV units. VC model elements of high‐powered incentives, autonomous organization, investment syndication, investment staging, and specialization were each associated with higher CV unit performance. Critically, different elements of the VC model predicted improvements in either strategic or financial performance in CV units. Survival of the CV unit was strongly associated with employing the VC model, partially mediated by enhanced performance. Copyright © 2009 Strategic Management Society.

75. Horizontal alliances as an alternative to autonomous production: product expansion mode choice in the worldwide aircraft industry 1945–2000

Strategic Management Journal 2009-04-03 Available
JournalStrategic Management Journal
Publication date2009-04-03
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1002/smj.770
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Bernard GarretteHEC Paris; HEC, Paris, France
Xavier CastañerUniversity of Lausanne; HEC Lausanne, Faculty of Business and Economics, University of Lausanne, Lausanne, Switzerland
Pierre DussaugeHEC Paris; HEC, Paris, France
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Abstract This study investigates why firms choose to undertake product expansion through alliances with competitors rather than on their own. We highlight product heterogeneity as a determinant of this make or ally choice. We propose that firms turn to horizontal alliances in order to implement product expansion projects that require greater resources than those available to them. More precisely, we hypothesize that a firm is more likely to launch a new product through a horizontal alliance rather than autonomously when the resource requirements of the project are greater, the resources available to the firm are more limited, there is a mismatch between resource endowment and requirement, and the firm's collaborative competence allows it to better cope with the interorganizational concerns that collaboration with competitors raises. We find support for our arguments on a sample of 310 new aircraft developments launched between 1945 and 2000, either by a single prime contractor or as a horizontal alliance in which prime contractorship is shared with another industry incumbent. Copyright © 2009 John Wiley &amp; Sons, Ltd.

KeywordsInnovation and Knowledge Management; Defense, Military, and Policy Studies; Economic Growth and Productivity; Competitor analysis; Alliance; Industrial organization; Product (mathematics); Business; Endowment; Resource (disambiguation); New product development; Marketing; Order (exchange); Economics; Diversification (marketing strategy); Competence (human resources); Computer science; Management; Finance; Geometry; Epistemology; Mathematics; Philosophy; Computer network; Law; Political science

76. Collegial Entrepreneurialism

Public Management Review 2009-05-01 Available
JournalPublic Management Review
Publication date2009-05-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/14719030902798248
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Suzanne RyanSchlumberger (Ireland); [email removed]
James GuthrieGuthrie Foundation; [email removed]
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

In recent decades, Australian universities have corporatized. Encouraged by government policies, universities adopted modernization practices that have been widely questioned. ‘Collegial entrepreneurialism’ is an approach that builds on collegial processes to protect academic values from the excesses of modernization. This paper examines the perceptions of business academics about the impacts of modernization in autonomous graduate schools of business. The research was undertaken prior to the most recent wave of higher education reform under which most of these schools disappeared. Their experience of three consequences of modernization, ‘hard’ managerialism, academic consumerism and fragmentation of work, provides insight into whether collegiality and academic values can exist within an entrepreneurial academic unit. Results indicate that overt manifestations of modernization are not threatening to ‘collegial entrepreneurialism’. However, in the absence of academic leadership, the more covert influences of consumerism and fragmentation pose a threat to the survival of ‘collegial entrepreneurialism’.

KeywordsHigher Education Governance and Development; Entrepreneurship Studies and Influences; Higher Education and Employability; Political science; Sociology; Business
Matched conceptsautonomous
Matched fieldsabstract

77. Investigating the ‘NPM-ness’ of agencies in Italy and Flanders

Public Management Review 2009-05-01 Available
JournalPublic Management Review
Publication date2009-05-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/14719030902798271
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Bram VerschuereSchlumberger (Ireland); [email removed]
Dario BarbieriUniversidad Iberoamericana; [email removed]
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Abstract Autonomous bodies established by or reformed under NPM-inspired reforms should be, from a normative point of view, characterized by (1) structural disaggregation, (2) managerial freedom, (3) contractual relationships with the oversight authorities, and (4) tasks on the operations side of the policy-operations divide. We aim to investigate the extent to which real-life agencies reflect these normative characteristics, by taking as an empirical setting the Flemish and Italian public sectors. Our findings suggest that in reality, the normative NPM ideal type agency is only rarely observed. Secondly, we want to explore the effect of the country, the age, and the tasks of the agency on the extent to which it reflects the characteristics of the NPM ideal-type. We find that Italian agencies better reflect the ideal type compared to their Flemish counterparts, and that there is virtually no effect of agencies' age and tasks on the extent to which they reflect the NPM ideal typical agency.

KeywordsPublic Policy and Administration Research; Regulation and Compliance Studies; Social Policy and Reform Studies; Flemish; Normative; Agency (philosophy); Ideal type; Ideal (ethics); Political science; Public administration; Sociology; Positive economics; Economics; Social science; Law; History; Archaeology

Matched fieldsabstract

78. Enterprise contested: Betwixt and between the discourses of career and enterprise in a UK bank

Human Relations 2009-08-28 Available
JournalHuman Relations
Publication date2009-08-28
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1177/0018726709336499
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Darren McCabeKeele University; School of Economic and Management Studies, Keele University, UK,
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

This article analyses the ‘enterprise’ discourse (Miller &amp; Rose, 1990; Rose, 1989) that endeavours to reinvent employees as responsible, autonomous, self-regulating, customer-focused, team players. In this study of a major UK bank, the staff both endorsed and turned the enterprise discourse back on management and so the boundaries between dissent and consent are blurred. The case study highlights that enterprise does not arrive fully formed and can be a weapon of employees rather than simply a tool of those who seek to exercise power. It is argued that whilst enterprise is a contemporary discourse, it reproduces aspects of a much older ‘career’ (McKinlay, 2002; Tempest et al., 2004) discourse in UK financial services. The continuity and discontinuity between the two discourses fuelled resistance, whilst oiling and obscuring, the reproduction of enduring inequalities, that straddle both discourses.

KeywordsLabor Movements and Unions; Management and Organizational Studies; Family Business Performance and Succession; Dissent; Resistance (ecology); Sociology; Devolution (biology); Power (physics); Public relations; Political science; Law; Politics; Human evolution; Physics; Biology; Ecology; Anthropology; Quantum mechanics

79. Attentional sensitization of unconscious cognition: Task sets modulate subsequent masked semantic priming.

Journal of Experimental Psychology General 2010-01-01 Available
JournalJournal of Experimental Psychology General
Publication date2010-01-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1037/a0019561
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Markus KieferUniversität Ulm; Department of Psychiatry, Univerity of Ulm, Ulm, Germany. [email removed]; Department of Psychiatry, Univerity of Ulm, Ulm, Germany
Ulla MartensUniversity of Ulm, Department of Psychiatry, Section for Cognitive Electrophysiology, Ulm, Germany
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

According to classical theories, automatic processes are autonomous and independent of higher level cognitive influence. In contrast, the authors propose that automatic processing depends on attentional sensitization of task-congruent processing pathways. In 3 experiments, the authors tested this hypothesis with a modified masked semantic priming paradigm during a lexical decision task by measuring event-related potentials (ERPs): Before masked prime presentation, participants attended an induction task either to semantic or perceptual stimulus features designed to activate a semantic or perceptual task set, respectively. Semantic priming effects on the N400 ERP component, an electrophysiological index of semantic processing, were obtained when a semantic task set was induced immediately before subliminal prime presentation, whereas a previously induced perceptual task set attenuated N400 priming. Across experiments, comparable results were obtained regardless of the difficulty level and the verbal or nonverbal nature of the induction tasks. In line with the proposed attentional sensitization model, unconscious semantic processing is enhanced by a semantic and attenuated by a perceptual task set. Hence, automatic processing of unconscious stimuli is susceptible to top-down control for optimizing goal-related information processing.

KeywordsNeural and Behavioral Psychology Studies; EEG and Brain-Computer Interfaces; Memory Processes and Influences; N400; Subliminal stimuli; Semantic memory; Cognitive psychology; Cognition; Psychology; Priming (agriculture); Perception; Lexical decision task; Event-related potential; Response priming; Repetition priming; Stimulus (psychology); Unconscious mind; Task (project management); Neuroscience; Management; Economics; Germination; Psychoanalysis; Biology; Botany
Matched conceptsautonomous
Matched fieldsabstract

80. When helping helps: Autonomous motivation for prosocial behavior and its influence on well-being for the helper and recipient.

Journal of Personality and Social Psychology 2010-01-20 Available
JournalJournal of Personality and Social Psychology
Publication date2010-01-20
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1037/a0016984
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Netta WeinsteinUniversity of Rochester; Department of Psychology, University of Rochester, Rochester, NY14627, USA. [email removed]
Richard M. Ryannot available
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Self-determination theory posits that the degree to which a prosocial act is volitional or autonomous predicts its effect on well-being and that psychological need satisfaction mediates this relation. Four studies tested the impact of autonomous and controlled motivation for helping others on well-being and explored effects on other outcomes of helping for both helpers and recipients. Study 1 used a diary method to assess daily relations between prosocial behaviors and helper well-being and tested mediating effects of basic psychological need satisfaction. Study 2 examined the effect of choice on motivation and consequences of autonomous versus controlled helping using an experimental design. Study 3 examined the consequences of autonomous versus controlled helping for both helpers and recipients in a dyadic task. Finally, Study 4 manipulated motivation to predict helper and recipient outcomes. Findings support the idea that autonomous motivation for helping yields benefits for both helper and recipient through greater need satisfaction. Limitations and implications are discussed.

KeywordsMotivation and Self-Concept in Sports; Behavioral Health and Interventions; Psychological Well-being and Life Satisfaction; Prosocial behavior; Psychology; Helping behavior; Social psychology; Self-determination theory; Task (project management); Well-being; Autonomy; Psychotherapist; Law; Management; Political science; Economics
Matched conceptsautonomous
Matched fieldstitle

81. It's the Connections: The Network Perspective in Interorganizational Research

Academy of Management Perspectives 2010-02-01 Available
JournalAcademy of Management Perspectives
Publication date2010-02-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.5465/amp.24.1.62
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Akbar ZaheerStrategic Analysis (United States); * Akbar Zaheer () is Professor, Curtis L. Carlson Chair in Strategic Management, and Director of the Strategic Management Research Center at the Carlson School of Management, University of Minnesota
Remzi GözübüyükIE University; Remzi Gözübüyük () is Assistant Professor at IE Business School, Madrid, Spain
Hana MilanovIE University; Hana Milanov () is Assistant Professor at IE Business School, Madrid, Spain
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Executive Overview The application of social network analysis to interorganizational contexts has seen an explosion of interest in the past several years. We argue that not only does the network or structural perspective add explanatory power to scholarly understanding of organizations' behavior and outcomes, but that it expands the universe of observed phenomena from an autonomous to a relational view for studying and explaining organizational action and outcomes. We develop an organizing framework based on three levels of network analysis (the dyad, the ego, and the whole network) and four theoretical mechanisms (resource access, trust, power/control, and signaling) to organize and review the key findings and debates in the interorganizational network literature. We point to avenues for future research based on the linkages across the boxes in our framework, gaps in the framework, and finally, extensions beyond the framework.

KeywordsBusiness Strategy and Innovation; Innovation and Knowledge Management; Management and Organizational Studies; Dyad; Perspective (graphical); Organizational network analysis; Knowledge management; Social network analysis; Explanatory power; Resource (disambiguation); Organization studies; Organizational behavior; Organizational structure; Power (physics); Sociology; Resource dependence theory; Social network (sociolinguistics); Key (lock); Computer science; Management; Psychology; Social capital; Social psychology; Epistemology; Organizational learning; Social science; Computer security; Quantum mechanics; Philosophy; Social media; Artificial intelligence; World Wide Web; Computer network; Physics; Economics
Matched conceptsautonomous
Matched fieldsabstract

Executive Overview The application of social network analysis to interorganizational contexts has seen an explosion of interest in the past several years. We argue that not only does the network or structural perspective add explanatory power to scholarly understanding of organizations' behavior and outcomes, but that it expands the universe of observed phenomena from an autonomous to a relational view for studying and explaining organizational action and outcomes. We develop an organizing framework based on three levels of network analysis (the dyad, the ego, and the whole network) and four theoretical mechanisms (resource access, trust, power/control, and signaling) to organize and review the key findings and debates in the interorganizational network literature. We point to avenues for future research based on the linkages across the boxes in our framework, gaps in the framework, and finally, extensions beyond the framework.

83. Rethinking Agency Theory: The View from Law

Academy of Management Review 2010-04-01 Available
JournalAcademy of Management Review
Publication date2010-04-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.5465/amr.35.2.zok294
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Luh Luh LanNational University of Singapore; University of Warwick; National University of SingaporeUniversity of Warwick
Loizos HeracleousUniversity of Warwick; National University of Singapore; National University of SingaporeUniversity of Warwick
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

We draw from legal theory to offer a fundamental rethinking of agency theory along three key dimensions: redefining the principal from shareholders to the corporation, redefining the status of the board from shareholders' agents to autonomous fiduciaries, and redefining the role of the board from monitors to mediating hierarchs. These dimensions contrast with classic agency theory, offering novel conceptions that can inform further theorization and empirical research in corporate governance.

KeywordsCorporate Finance and Governance; Corporate Insolvency and Governance; Law, Economics, and Judicial Systems; Corporation; Agency (philosophy); Corporate governance; Principal–agent problem; Shareholder; Principal (computer security); Corporate law; Law and economics; Key (lock); Sociology; Organizational theory; Empirical research; Agency cost; Business; Law; Political science; Accounting; Public relations; Management; Epistemology; Economics; Social science; Computer science; Computer security; Philosophy; Operating system

84. Why and How Will a Group Act Autonomously to Make an Impact on the Development of Organizational Capabilities?

Journal of Management Studies 2010-05-18 Available
JournalJournal of Management Studies
Publication date2010-05-18
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-6486.2010.00952.x
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Krsto PandžaUniversity of Leeds; Leeds University Business School; Leeds University Business School ,
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

abstract In this paper I report the findings of an inductive, interpretative case study of proactive and autonomous actions instigated by a group at a major pharmaceutical firm in order to accelerate and shape organizational capability at this firm. This two‐year field research was seen as an ideal vehicle for investigating why a group within a firm proactively engages with a pattern of capability development, how such proactive engagement is conducted, and what these proactive activities are. I assert that autonomous action originates from intra‐firm heterogeneity of group‐level cognitive frames and social identities. The evidence suggests that a group with a particularly distinct perception of the strategic value of a capability will be more likely to initiate autonomous action with the aim of making an impact on capability development. The likelihood of autonomous action increases further if a group acts to strengthen the distinctiveness of its own identity by raising the perceived value of a capability that compares unfavourably with other firms' capabilities. The field observations suggest that in circumstances of high inter‐group dependency and limited group authority, the group attempts to make an impact on capability development by adopting creative and socially complex framing practices. The group formalizes a collective and cognitive search process in order to legitimize the preferred action and subtly sells the issue to higher authority without causing conflict, while still sustaining the group's intent.

KeywordsInnovation and Knowledge Management; Management and Organizational Studies; Business Strategy and Innovation; Optimal distinctiveness theory; Framing (construction); Collective action; Action (physics); Perception; Business; Cognition; Order (exchange); Social psychology; Value (mathematics); Social identity theory; Marketing; Public relations; Psychology; Social group; Political science; Computer science; Engineering; Finance; Physics; Machine learning; Structural engineering; Politics; Law; Neuroscience; Quantum mechanics
Matched conceptsautonomous
Matched fieldsabstract

85. World Society, Institutional Theories, and the Actor

Annual Review of Sociology 2010-06-01 Available
JournalAnnual Review of Sociology
Publication date2010-06-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1146/annurev.soc.012809.102506
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
John W. MeyerStanford University; Department of Sociology, Stanford University, Stanford, California 94305;
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Much modern social theory depicts society as made up of autonomous and purposive individual and organized actors. In reaction, the new institutional theories build arguments about the wider social conditions supporting stable systems of such agentic actors. Phenomenological versions, which are especially relevant to analyses of modern integrating but stateless world society, treat actor identities as themselves constructed in the wider and now global cultural context. These ideas call attention to the modern collective construction of expansive models of actors, the rapid diffusion and adoption of elaborated models of actor agency and rights, the consequently decoupled character of actor identities and activities in the modern system, and the extraordinary mobilizing potential built into the elaborated models of individual and organizational actors in world society and into the inconsistencies between these models and activity.

KeywordsSocial Policy and Reform Studies; Critical Theory and Philosophy; Management and Organizational Studies; Expansive; Sociology; Agency (philosophy); Context (archaeology); Stateless protocol; Epistemology; Civil society; Political science; Politics; Social science; Law; Computer science; State (computer science); Materials science; Composite material; Algorithm; Biology; Paleontology; Philosophy; Compressive strength

86. The Cost of Self-Protection: Threat Response and Performance as a Function of Autonomous and Controlled Motivations

Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin 2010-08-01 Available
JournalPersonality and Social Psychology Bulletin
Publication date2010-08-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1177/0146167210375618
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Holley S. HodginsSkidmore College; Skidmore College, Saratoga Springs, NY, USA,; Psychology Department, Skidmore College, Saratoga Springs, NY 12866, USA
Kristin S. WeibustUniversity of Vermont; University of Vermont, VT, USA
Netta WeinsteinVA Boston Healthcare System; National Center for PTSD; National Center for Post Traumatic Stress Disorder; Universität Hamburg, Germany, National Center for PTSD, Women's Health Sciences Division, VA Boston Healthcare System, Boston, MA, USA
Sara ShiffmanSkidmore College; Skidmore College, Saratoga Springs, NY, USA
Anita MillerSkidmore College; Skidmore College, Saratoga Springs, NY, USA
Garth CoombsSkidmore College; Skidmore College, Saratoga Springs, NY, USA
Kathryn C. AdairVA Boston Healthcare System; National Center for PTSD; National Center for PTSD, Women's Health Sciences Division, VA Boston Healthcare System, Boston, MA, USA
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Seventy-seven undergraduates, primed for autonomous or controlled motivation, were videotaped and physiologically monitored during a stressful interview and subsequent speech. Interview videotapes were coded for behavioral measures of threat response; speech videotapes were coded for performance. It was hypothesized that relative to controlled motivation, autonomous motivation would decrease interview threat response and enhance speech performance, and that threat response would mediate the effect of motivation on performance. Results support the prediction across measures of verbal, paralinguistic, smiling, vocal fundamental frequency, and cardiovascular response. Autonomously primed participants continued to show less cardiovascular threat throughout the later speech and gave better speeches. Finally, speech performance was mediated by interview threat response. Results demonstrate that relative to controlled motivation, autonomous motivation lowers threat response, which enhances performance.

KeywordsBullying, Victimization, and Aggression; Social and Intergroup Psychology; Communication in Education and Healthcare; Psychology; Paralanguage; Social psychology; Function (biology); Cognitive psychology; Communication; Evolutionary biology; Biology
Matched conceptsautonomous
Matched fieldstitle

87. Academic Influence amongst the UK Public Elite

Sociology 2010-08-01 Available
JournalSociology
Publication date2010-08-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1177/0038038510369365
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Dave GriffithsUniversity of Stirling; University of Stirling,
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

This article examines the position of academics within UK quasi-autonomous decision-making. Data have been collected on 2858 individuals, sitting on the boards of 187 Westminster quangos, to assess which social groups and outside institutions hold influence on independent decision-making. These include 200 academics, sitting on 84 different boards. Analysis of the networks generated through mutual memberships of outside institutions shows academics are sitting in strategic positions within the quango sector. This article argues that higher academics have retained their high public position. It utilizes social network analysis to demonstrate that academics hold strategic positions within governance networks, signifying both political influence and associations with the social elite.

KeywordsHigher Education Governance and Development; Social and Cultural Dynamics; Social Capital and Networks; Elite; Position (finance); Corporate governance; Politics; Sociology; Sitting; Public relations; Political science; Public administration; Management; Economics; Law; Medicine; Pathology; Finance
Matched conceptsautonomous
Matched fieldsabstract

88. Deliberate Learning to Improve Performance in Dynamic Service Settings: Evidence from Hospital Intensive Care Units

Organization Science 2010-09-16 Available
JournalOrganization Science
Publication date2010-09-16
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1287/orsc.1100.0570
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Ingrid M. NembhardYale University; Yale University, School of Medicine and School of Management, New Haven, Connecticut 06520; Yale University, School of Medicine and School of Management, New Haven, Connecticut 06520#TAB#
Anita L. TuckerHarvard Business School, Boston, Massachusetts 02163; Harvard Business School, Boston, Massachusetts 02163 ;
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Dynamic service settings—characterized by workers who interact with customers to deliver services in a rapidly changing, uncertain, and complex environment (e.g., hospitals)—play an important role in the economy. Organizational learning studies in these settings have largely investigated autonomous learning via cumulative experience as a strategy for performance improvement. Whether induced learning through the use of deliberate learning activities provides additional performance benefits has been neglected. We argue that the use of deliberate learning activities offers performance benefits beyond those of cumulative experience because these activities counter the learning challenges presented by rapid knowledge growth, uncertainty, and complexity in dynamic settings. We test whether there are additional performance benefits to using deliberate learning activities and whether the effectiveness of these activities depends on interdisciplinary collaboration in the workgroup. We test our hypotheses in a study of 23 hospital neonatal intensive care units (NICUs) involved in a quality improvement collaborative. We find that using deliberate learning activities is associated with better workgroup performance, as measured by NICUs' risk-adjusted mortality rates for 2159 infant patients, but only after two years. In the shorter term, using these activities is associated with worse performance. By the third year, the positive impact of using deliberate learning activities is similar to the benefit of cumulative experience (18% and 20% reduction in odds of mortality, respectively). Contrary to prediction, interdisciplinary collaboration mediates, rather than moderates, the relationship between using deliberate learning activities and workgroup performance. Thus, our data suggest that using deliberate learning activities fosters interdisciplinary collaboration.

KeywordsComplex Systems and Decision Making; Construction Project Management and Performance; Innovative Approaches in Technology and Social Development; Workgroup; Odds; Service (business); Test (biology); Psychology; Organizational learning; Intensive care; Knowledge management; Business; Medicine; Marketing; Computer science; Biology; Computer network; Internal medicine; Logistic regression; Paleontology; Intensive care medicine
Matched conceptsautonomous
Matched fieldsabstract

89. Family Displays and Personal Lives

Sociology 2010-12-01 Available
JournalSociology
Publication date2010-12-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1177/0038038510381612
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Allison JamesUniversity of Sheffield; University of Sheffield, UK,
Penny CurtisUniversity of Sheffield; University of Sheffield, UK,
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

In this article we explore how Finch’s (2007) concept of display might illuminate the new sociology of personal life as set out by Smart (2007). Drawing on narratives of family life and eating practices, from both parents and children, the article considers how these work as tools of family display. For some families, displays are used to affirm an idiosyncratic sense of their family through drawing on particular cultural motifs of ‘family’. For other families, practices of display work to smooth over challenges from within. Displays can also be more normative, confirming wider hegemonic models of what the family is. Thus, while displays — and therefore ideas of family — do take different forms, nonetheless they also demonstrate that, as Smart suggests, people’s personal lives need always to be understood as embedded in particular social and cultural worlds, rather than a function of lone, autonomous individuals.

KeywordsGender, Feminism, and Media; Children's Rights and Participation; Child Welfare and Adoption; Normative; Sociology; Narrative; Function (biology); Set (abstract data type); Family life; Social psychology; Aesthetics; Hegemony; Epistemology; Gender studies; Psychology; Politics; Computer science; Philosophy; Law; Political science; Linguistics; Evolutionary biology; Biology; Programming language

Matched fieldsabstract

90. Risks of Corporate Entrepreneurship: Autonomy and Agency Issues

Organization Science 2011-02-24 Available
JournalOrganization Science
Publication date2011-02-24
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1287/orsc.1110.0645
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Katsuhiko ShimizuKeio University; Graduate School of Business, Keio University, Yokohama Kanagawa, Japan 223-8526; Graduate School of Business, Keio University, Yokohama Kanagawa, Japan 223-8526#TAB#
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Although research on corporate entrepreneurship (CE) has attracted increasing attention, risks of CE are not well examined. In this paper, we examine direct and indirect agency risks associated with providing middle and operational managers with autonomy in the hopes of encouraging CE. The key ideas behind our discussion involve two dilemmas of CE. (1) To solve the risk-averseness problem of middle and operational managers and unleash their entrepreneurial ideas, top management needs to encourage autonomous behaviors, which can also exacerbate a different type of agency problem: opportunistic behaviors. (2) Although the generation of new ideas to explore new strategic directions and/or opportunities is important, the very newness of the ideas make them difficult to evaluate, and thus the selection process may be perceived as unfair by some managers. We propose that (a) stock options, (b) passive monitoring, and (c) perceived procedural justice would attenuate the negative side effects associated with CE, such as opportunistic behaviors and perceived unfairness. Although research in CE often stresses autonomy of middle and operational managers for new idea generation as important, examining the whole process in terms of idea generation, selection, and implementation with respect to potential risks will extend the CE literature.

KeywordsEntrepreneurship Studies and Influences; Corporate Finance and Governance; Corporate Social Responsibility Reporting; Autonomy; Agency (philosophy); Entrepreneurship; Business; Process (computing); Middle management; Principal–agent problem; Public relations; Marketing; Knowledge management; Sociology; Corporate governance; Finance; Political science; Computer science; Operating system; Social science; Law

91. Young people’s aspirations for education, work, family and leisure

Work Employment and Society 2011-03-01 Available
JournalWork Employment and Society
Publication date2011-03-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1177/0950017010389242
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Paula McDonaldQueensland University of Technology; Queensland University of Technology, Australia; Queensland University of Technology AUSTRALIA
Barbara PiniCurtin University; Curtin University, Australia; Curtin University, Australia#TAB#
Janis BaileyGriffith University; Griffith University, Australia; Griffith Univ. (Australia)
Robin PriceQueensland University of Technology; Queensland University of Technology, Australia; Queensland University of Technology AUSTRALIA
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Young people are arguably facing more ‘complex and contested’ transitions to adulthood and an increasing array of ‘non-linear’ paths. Education and training have been extended, identity is increasingly shaped through leisure and consumerism and youth must navigate their life trajectories in highly individualised ways. The study utilises 819 short essays compiled by students aged 14–16 years from 19 schools in Australia. It examines how young people understand their own unique positions and the possibilities open to them through their aspirations and future orientations to employment and family life. These young people do not anticipate postponing work identities, but rather embrace post-school options such as gaining qualifications, work experience and achieving financial security. Boys expected a distant involvement in family life secondary to participation in paid work. In contrast, around half the girls simultaneously expected a future involving primary care-giving and an autonomous, independent career, suggesting attempts to remake gendered inequalities.

KeywordsYouth Education and Societal Dynamics; Education Systems and Policy; Gender, Feminism, and Media; Consumerism; Youth work; Work (physics); Family life; Inequality; Identity (music); Sociology; Leisure time; Gender studies; Public relations; Political science; Physical activity; Physical medicine and rehabilitation; Mathematical analysis; Physics; Mechanical engineering; Medicine; Mathematics; Engineering; Acoustics; Law

92. Emotional Life on the Market Frontier

Annual Review of Sociology 2011-03-02 Available
JournalAnnual Review of Sociology
Publication date2011-03-02
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1146/annurev-soc-081309-150137
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Arlie Russell HochschildUniversity of California, Berkeley; Department of Sociology, University of California, Berkeley, California 94729;
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

As American society has become ever more dominated by the market, sociological interest in commodification has paradoxically declined. Marx, among others, noted how a worker can become estranged from his work—the doing of it, the tools of it, and the product resulting from it. Consumers can become estranged from all these, too. As workers and consumers today, we often detach ourselves from what we make and buy, and extreme forms of detachment we can call estrangement or alienation. Marx's iconic worker was (a) the nineteenth-century male factory worker for whom (b) estrangement was a static state (c) about which the victim had no narrative. In today's economy, we can look to the female service worker who does emotional labor to alter her state of estrangement and whose narrative may be that of “free choice.” Is the commercial surrogate I met in a for-profit clinic in India an autonomous agent in a free market, I wondered, or is she the latest version of Marx's “alienated man”? This essay grapples with that question.

KeywordsPolitical Economy and Marxism; Commodification; Alienation; Frontier; Narrative; Sociology; State (computer science); Capitalism; Profit (economics); Anomie; Aesthetics; Neoclassical economics; Market economy; Economics; Political science; Law; Art; Politics; Literature; Computer science; Algorithm; Anthropology

Matched fieldsabstract

93. Exploring and Explaining Patterns of Adaptation and Selection in Corporate Environmental Strategy in the USA

Organization Studies 2011-04-01 Available
JournalOrganization Studies
Publication date2011-04-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1177/0170840611403670
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Frederik DahlmannUniversity of Bath; University of Bath, UK,
Stephen BrammerUniversity of Warwick; University of Warwick, UK
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

While the societal salience of environmental issues appears to have risen substantially in recent years, relatively little is known regarding how environmental responsiveness has evolved over time within business in general, or within particular private sector organizations. In this study we seek to address this deficit by exploring the evolving pattern of environmental strategy within a large sample of US companies over the period 1997 to 2006. Based on evolutionary theories of selection and adaptation as well as Burgelman’s strategic change processes, we characterize the pattern of evolving responses to natural environmental issues, distinguish between changes in the overall attention paid to environmental issues that are attributable to the forces of adaptation and selection, and examine the distinctive characteristics of those organizations that have exhibited the most dramatic improvements in their environmental strategy. Our findings indicate that while there is widespread inertia in respect of environmental responsiveness within our sample of companies, those companies that have achieved significant improvements in their environmental strategies do so as a result of a combination of autonomous and induced change processes. Such companies have often recently appointed a new CEO, are relatively strongly engaged in research and development, and have strong prior achievements in environmental strategy.

KeywordsEnvironmental Sustainability in Business; Environmental Education and Sustainability; Sustainable Supply Chain Management; Salience (neuroscience); Adaptation (eye); Environmental change; Selection (genetic algorithm); Sample (material); Natural selection; Ecology; Climate change; Psychology; Biology; Chromatography; Cognitive psychology; Chemistry; Neuroscience; Computer science; Artificial intelligence

94. Job Satisfaction in The Public Service

Public Management Review 2011-04-01 Available
JournalPublic Management Review
Publication date2011-04-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/14719037.2010.532959
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Jeannette TaylorUniversity of Western Australia; Political Science and International Relations , University of Western Australia , Crawley, Australia E-mail: [email removed]
Jonathan WestoverUtah Valley University; Woodbury School of Business , Utah Valley University , Orem, UT, USA E-mail: [email removed]
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Abstract What satisfies a public servant? Is it the money? Or is it something else, like an interesting and autonomous job, or serving the public interest? Utilizing non-panel longitudinal data from the International Social Survey Program on Work Orientations across different countries for 1997 and 2005, this article examines the effects of a selection of antecedents that are commonly related to job satisfaction. The respondents from different countries were found to share similarities in terms of what satisfies them in their jobs. The emphasis placed on these factors was however found to vary for some countries.

95. Accounting for Consent: Exploring the Reproduction of the Labour Process

Sociology 2011-06-01 Available
JournalSociology
Publication date2011-06-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1177/0038038511399625
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Darren McCabeKeele University; Keele University, UK,; Keele University , UK
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

This article draws on the case of a UK bank to consider why branch employees tolerated conditions that impoverished their working lives. The article explores how we are defined as particular types of subject and how we turn ourselves into subjects. The particular focus is on how we simultaneously understand ourselves as economic, cultural and autonomous subjects and how this contributes to the production of consent. It is argued that our propensity to adopt complex and shifting identities, in relation to a variety of discourses, engages and constitutes us as consenting subjects.

96. Identities, Discipline and Routines

Organization Studies 2011-07-01 Available
JournalOrganization Studies
Publication date2011-07-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1177/0170840611407018
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Andrew D. BrownUniversity of Warwick; University of Warwick, UK,; #N# University of Warwick, UK
Michael LewisUniversity of Bath; University of Bath, UK; University Of Bath, UK
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

This paper analyses how people’s subjectively construed identities are disciplined by, and appropriated from, their talk about organizational routines. Identity work, we argue, is not just an expression of agency but also of power. Based on a study of a UK regional law firm, our research counter-balances understandings of professional lawyers as autonomous knowledge-workers, and emphasizes instead the extent of their subjection to disciplinary processes. It shows that power is intrinsic to discursive constructions of routine processes of organizing. We examine lawyers’ accounts of their time-keeping and billing routine, and how these both fabricated their identities, and how individuals said that they confronted, shifted and perverted organizationally sanctioned systems of meaning. The research contribution of this paper is to examine empirically and to theorize how discourse about routines both disciplines and is a resource for identity work.

KeywordsManagement and Organizational Studies; Legal Education and Practice Innovations; Law in Society and Culture; Agency (philosophy); Discipline; Identity (music); Sociology; Meaning (existential); Power (physics); Organization studies; Epistemology; Work (physics); Resource (disambiguation); Public relations; Social science; Political science; Computer science; Aesthetics; Engineering; Quantum mechanics; Mechanical engineering; Physics; Philosophy; Computer network

97. Innovation Types and Network Relationships

Entrepreneurship Theory and Practice 2011-07-04 Available
JournalEntrepreneurship Theory and Practice
Publication date2011-07-04
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1111/j.1540-6520.2011.00474.x
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Jukka PartanenAalto University; Aalto University, School of Economics (Department of Marketing)
Sylvie ChettyUppsala University; Massey University and Research Associate in the Department of Business Studies, Uppsala University in Sweden
Arto RajalaAalto University; Aalto University, School of Economics (Department of Marketing)
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

We examine network relationships, particularly the type and strength of these relationships and how firms use them in order to commercialize different types of innovations. We conduct an in–depth case study of four small firms to provide insight on how and why a portfolio of network relationships (e.g., suppliers, distributors, customers, and research institutes) helps small new innovative firms to gain resources. We develop a framework of four innovation types and expand on how successful commercialization for each innovation type requires certain types and strength of relationships. Both types (systemic and autonomous) of radical innovations require strong collaborative ties with customers, whereas incremental innovations are commercialized through different types of downstream networks.

KeywordsInnovation and Knowledge Management; Business Strategy and Innovation; Entrepreneurship Studies and Influences; Business; Commercialization; Order (exchange); Portfolio; Industrial organization; Marketing; Knowledge management; Downstream (manufacturing); Computer science; Finance
