Matched fieldsabstract

98. Workaholic and work engaged employees: Dead ringers or worlds apart?

Journal of Occupational Health Psychology 2011-07-25 Available
JournalJournal of Occupational Health Psychology
Publication date2011-07-25
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1037/a0024392
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Ilona van BeekUtrecht University; Department of Work and Organizational Psychology, Utrecht University, Utrecht, The Netherlands. [email removed]
Toon W. TarisUtrecht University; (Department of Work and Organizational Psychology, Utrecht University, Utrecht, Netherlands)
Wilmar B. SchaufeliUtrecht University; (Department of Work and Organizational Psychology, Utrecht University, Utrecht, Netherlands)
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Building on Deci and Ryan's Self-Determination Theory and Meijman and Mulder's Effort-Recovery Model, the present study examined the nature, antecedents, and consequences of working hard (i.e., workaholism and work engagement) in a Dutch convenience sample of 1,246 employees. A confirmatory factor analysis showed that workaholism and work engagement were two largely independent concepts. Crossing these two concepts yielded four types of workers: workaholic employees, engaged employees, engaged workaholics, and nonworkaholic/nonengaged employees. MANOVA and subsequent ANOVAs were used to compare these four groups regarding their motivation, working hours, and levels of burnout. As expected, study results revealed that workaholic employees were driven by controlled motivation, whereas engaged employees were driven by autonomous motivation. Engaged workaholics were driven by both controlled and autonomous motivation. In addition, the results revealed that engaged workaholics spent most time on working. Unlike workaholic employees, engaged workaholics did not experience the highest levels of burnout, suggesting that high engagement may buffer the adverse consequences of workaholism. The present study emphasizes the importance of differentiating among at least three categories of employees who work hard: workaholic employees, engaged employees, and-for the first time-engaged workaholics.

KeywordsWorkaholism, burnout, and well-being; Perfectionism, Procrastination, Anxiety Studies; Work-Family Balance Challenges; Psychology; Burnout; Multivariate analysis of variance; Deci-; Work engagement; Person–environment fit; Social psychology; Occupational burnout; Confirmatory factor analysis; Work (physics); Sample (material); Applied psychology; Clinical psychology; Emotional exhaustion; Structural equation modeling; Chemistry; Law; Autonomy; Political science; Statistics; Machine learning; Engineering; Chromatography; Mechanical engineering; Computer science; Mathematics
Matched conceptsautonomous
Matched fieldsabstract

99. Management Research in the UK: A Personal View

British Journal of Management 2011-08-19 Available
JournalBritish Journal of Management
Publication date2011-08-19
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-8551.2011.00768.x
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Cary L. CooperLancaster University; Lancaster University Ghana; Lancaster University Management School, University of Lancaster, Lancaster LA1 4YW, UKEmail: [email protected]; Lancaster University Management School, University of Lancaster, Lancaster LA1 4YW, UK Email: [email removed]
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Thomas Jefferson once reflected that ‘the past is prologue’, so to understand the current state and future development of management research in the UK we have to briefly explore its origins. The academic field of management in UK higher education has a very recent history, in contrast with many other academic disciplines. Indeed, the first academic presence of management in the UK was only in l917, at the seat of the industrial revolution, Manchester. The Manchester Mechanics Institution established the Department of Industrial Administration, whose role was specifically to train engineers and scientists for potential future managerial roles, with little if any research infrastructure or strategy. This Institute metamorphosed into the University of Manchester Institute of Science and Technology in 1966 (as an autonomous body but also as a Faculty of the University of Manchester), at around the same time as the Foundation of Management Education was established to create two UK centres of excellence in management education, the London Business School and the Manchester Business School – both created through private sector funding in 1964. From 1964 onwards, the number and range of academic development in the management sciences escalated to the point that by the 2009 Research Assessment Exercise (RAE) there were over 100 business and management schools in higher education institutes, producing thousands of undergraduates, MBAs and PhDs. The field went through what Preston and Post (1974) described as the three stages of managerial development, which they referred to as the ‘three managerial revolutions’. The first managerial revolution was the appearance of management itself as a specialized function within hierarchical organizations. The second revolution, ‘professionalization’, was encouraged by the growth in industrial organizations and the complexity of managerial tasks. The third, ‘participation’, was ‘the inclusion of persons and groups involved and concerned with the diverse outcomes of managerial activity as participants in the managerial process’. It is this third revolution that led to the importance of research, particularly in systematically evaluating what worked and what did not work in this managerial process. There has been a longer history of research in a limited number of subjects allied and/or integral to management, such as economics, finance, occupational psychology, operational research and the like, but as new functional areas of interest emerged in management (i.e. marketing, human resource management), research was given a new impetus, stimulating interdisciplinary work between disciplines and functions within management and between the management sciences and other social science disciplines. But the real sea change occurred with the advent of the first RAE in the 1980s. This process of rewarding universities for research output directly had the effect of encouraging universities to recruit and develop their researchers, and in the naturally competitive world of business schools it had an even more profound impact of galvanizing researchers to publish their work in the best international journals. There were not only the individual incentives in terms of career advancement through their research output, but also by enhancing their research portfolio it allowed them to be more attractive to higher rated business schools and/or universities – it gave them, in essence, greater career mobility. From the mid-1980s until the last RAE in 2009, the quantity and quality of research did improve enormously, with many British management academics now publishing in more highly ranked international journals and working more collaboratively with academics from other countries. I was fortunate enough to be a member of the RAE panel in management and business in 1991, and then Chair of this panel in 1996 and 2001. I could see how incentive-based approaches, like the RAE in the UK, could motivate both business schools and their academics to invest more effort in applying for research grants, working collaboratively with colleagues on communal research projects, taking greater risks in publishing in more highly rated journals, attending more high profile international conferences (e.g. Academy of Management annual conference) and advancing their special research interests in the wider global community. By the 2001 RAE in the UK, however, the research culture had been changed so dramatically that ‘the research ethic’ was now embedded in the psyche of management researchers, no longer needing the incentives of an RAE that in earlier years stimulated aspiring researchers. Indeed, by 2001 and beyond, game theory overtook the more positive aspects of the RAE, as universities tried to find ways of manufacturing good results (by the proportion of research active staff they put in, by creating joint authorships which were in name only etc.). So the RAE had achieved its goals by the turn of the millennium and in my view no longer served any useful purpose, even in equitably distributing government research funding for the higher education sector (with the science and technology subjects getting a disproportionate allocation compared with the social sciences, the arts and humanities). So the good news is that the UK's research base in the various management disciplines is vibrant, and now part of the DNA of management and business school academics. So, what research is taking place and being published in the UK, and has this changed over the last couple of decades? I tentatively explored this by looking at the output of the British Journal of Management (BJM) from 1990 to 1995 and comparing it with papers published in 2005–2010. I did a rough categorization of papers into a number of different functional areas like ‘marketing’ or ‘organizational behaviour’ etc.; or by research topics like ‘mergers and acquisitions’, ‘training and development’, ‘strategic change’ etc. It was interesting to note that at the beginning of the life of BJM there were a large number of papers that were in ‘the disciplines’ that make up management, particularly marketing, macro organizational behaviour, operations management, accounting and strategy, as opposed to research topic based papers. Incidentally, this was very different from the papers that appeared in the Academy of Management journals of the time, which tended to include papers on organizational behaviour, strategy, management learning etc. but rarely included papers in disciplines outside of organizational behaviour, strategy, human resource management etc., such are marketing, accounting and operations management. Where there were papers in BJM on specific topic areas (illustrated by the titles of some of the BJM papers below), they were in mergers and acquisitions (e.g. ‘The impact of mergers and acquisitions on people at work’), strategic change (e.g. ‘Patterns of strategic change in the development of human resource management’) and management training and development (e.g. ‘Managing as a performing art’), with a smaller number on gender (e.g. ‘Men managers and women workers: women employees as an under-used resource’), organizational psychology or micro-organizational behaviour (e.g. ‘The content of the psychological contract’), entrepreneurship (e g. ‘What does it mean to be enterprising’) and others in the general management arena (e.g. ‘What is happening to middle management’). When we look at the most recent five-year period, from 2005 to 2010, we notice a decline in published papers in some of the disciplines of the earlier years, particularly marketing, accounting, operations management and macro-organizational behaviour, which may indicate the influence of the Academy of Management and/or the internationalization of BJM and/or the editor of the journal during these different sampling periods. I suspect it is basically about the maturity of the academic field, as well as its international orientation, which have broadened the discipline base of published papers. We now see a wider distribution of disciplines from human resource management to micro-organizational behaviour to public sector management to organizational theory, as well as the fields that were more prominent over 20 years ago but less visibly dominating the journal pages. When it comes to the specific topics of interest, once again there is a more even distribution of subject areas, but with interest still in mergers and acquisition, strategic change and change management and gender, but with a more expansive portfolio of topics (illustrated by some of the titles of papers in that topic area) such as cross-cultural issues (e.g. ‘Promotability of host-country nationals: a cross-cultural study’), ethics (e.g. ‘Business ethics as practice’), top management (e.g. ‘Top management, team heterogeneity, strategic change and operational performance’), corporate reputation (e.g. ‘Examining the antecedents and consequences of corporate reputation: a customer perspective’), corporate social responsibility (e.g. ‘Employee volunteering and social capital: contributions to corporate social responsibility), knowledge networks (‘The role of non-structural and informal mechanisms of integration and coordination as forces in knowledge creation’) and innovation (e.g. ‘How innovative are UK firms’) but the most surprising significant addition to the literature, but probably in retrospect in reality the least surprising, was the large number of published studies on leadership (Avolio, Walumbwa and Weber, 2009). This was the single largest category of specific research topic areas, but it was also frequently linked to one of the other issues above, as illustrated by papers entitled ‘Phases of the adoption of innovation in organizations: effects of environment, organization and top managers’ or ‘Leadership and effectiveness in the context of gender: the role of leaders’ verbal behaviour’ etc. Leadership, at all levels in an organization, is an important aspect of achieving any of the above, whether in terms of ethics or corporate reputation or corporate social responsibility (Rothstein and Burke, 2010). Also, given the economic downturn and recession, the issue of leadership has come to the fore, particularly in the finance sector, the lack of leadership in managing risk and growth and the need for a different management style as we enter ‘recession recovery’ mode. As Gadman and Cooper (2009) suggest the leadership challenge is that in this era of overwhelming complexity and ambiguity, contemporary leadership practices are failing to respond to this new world order because they are based on outdated assumptions about the way the world works. These tenets, which emphasize ‘power over and control of’ people, have outlived their usefulness. To seize today's rich patterns of opportunities, we need trust and openness, shared information, clusters of capabilities and a focus on teaming these capabilities through creative leadership. For me it is really heartening to see topics emerging from the literature which have real meaning for the times we live in. The management research community are more and more concerned about leadership; about how we can use the knowledge base and technology we have to improve performance and get better work–life balance; about innovation and creativity; about ethics, given Enron, the expenses debacle of UK politicians and the like; about how we manage effectively strategic change, whilst minimizing its impact on the health and well-being of employees; and about what we all need to do to give back to the community through corporate social responsibility and sustainability more generally. This trend may be partly a result of the importance of ‘research impact’, and its implications for future management research funding, but I would like to think that business school academics see the significance of making their work relevant, whether it is in theory-building or in their empirical investigations, because they have become more interested in the implications of their work for policy making and ultimately seeing their work translated into strategic and significant effective change (Cooper and Starkey, 2010). Much of what is done in the name of management research can positively contribute to the economy and to enhanced organizational performance. Burke and Cooper (2008) highlight a number of examples of management research that can positively influence organizational performance and the success of an economy. Sirota, Mischkind and Meltzer (2005) found that companies who have more enthusiastic and job-satisfied employees outperform the average stock market prices of other companies in their sector by more than two and a half times; and that companies with low job satisfaction and unenthusiastic employees lag behind their industry competitors' average price by five times. The authors found that the role of management was fundamental to levels of employee satisfaction and enthusiasm. Managers who engendered pride, camaraderie and equity among their employees produced the best performance. Leslie, Loch and Schaninger (2006) also explored the relationship of management practices in 230 businesses around the world, collecting data on more than 115,000 individual managers and employees. They concluded that three management practices were common among many of their high performing organizations: clarifying roles and accountabilities, articulating a compelling direction and vision for the future, and developing a strong performance-oriented culture based on openness and trust. Kotter (1996) and Kotter and Cohen's (2002) follow-up work found that successful management practices for large-scale change, in over 100 cases of major organizational restructurings, identified eight successful managerial behaviours: (1) increasing urgency for change among respected and influential employees; (2) building the guiding team for the change, those with credibility, skills and reputations; (3) creating the right vision; (4) communicating to all stakeholders for buy-in; (5) empowering all to action; (6) creating short-term wins; (7) being persistent, showing employees that the vision is being realized; and (8) perpetuating the change, making the new behaviours become part of the new culture. And finally, the notion that major structural change inevitably means downsizing and outsourcing was contradicted by Cascio's (2002) research involving an 18-year study of the Standard and Poor's 500 firms, showing that companies who restructured through downsizing were not only less profitable than those who did not, but tended to do far worse. Treating people as disposable assets, to exploit them when you need them but to dispense with them when you do not, has adverse consequences, not only to the health and self-esteem of individuals but also to the bottom line of the firm. All of these studies are examples of how management research can make a difference to the performance, productivity and well-being of organizations. This is evidence-based management, with a more focused purpose to provide the data and support organizations need, particularly during the onerous economic times we are going through. Weitzel (2009) sets the challenge for peak performing organizations in the future: unrelenting pressure for continuing change from technological innovation, and altered expectations of clients and customers, force high performance leaders to guide their organizations through each new set of pressures for change. High performance leaders recognize they must work with those they lead to make them part of the process, as origins and not pawns, from the earliest stages, through the planning for change and then through the completion to the next phase. The high performing organization operates as a team or set of teams. Clearly innovation and genius are important ingredients. But success in a high performance culture is the result of the actions of all. We, as management researchers, are part of that team, providing the ideas, evidence and good practice examples, as ammunition for change and development in the managerial and organizational arena – a challenge, never needed as much as in today's world of business. Cary L. Cooper, CBE, is Distinguished Professor of Organizational Psychology and Health at Lancaster University Management School, Chair of the Academy of Social Sciences and Founding President of the British Academy of Management.

KeywordsManagement Theory and Practice; Management and Organizational Studies; Accounting and Organizational Management; Excellence; Management; Prologue; Business history; Institution; Higher education; Sociology; Industrial Revolution; Political science; Public relations; Engineering ethics; Engineering; Social science; Economics; History; Law; Archaeology

Matched fieldsabstract

100. The Dialectical Sense of Humour: Routine Joking in a Taylorized Factory

Organization Studies 2011-10-01 Available
JournalOrganization Studies
Publication date2011-10-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1177/0170840611421256
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Marek KorczynskiLoughborough University; Loughborough University, UK
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Starting from the premise that context is central to understanding humour, the paper examines humour in three key contexts of the workplace – the labour process, control relations and peer relations. The paper argues that there have been important studies which have shown humour embedded in control relations and peer relations, but the way in which humour may be embedded in the labour process has not received the same sustained attention. The paper reports on a participant-observation ethnography in a Taylorized blinds factory in which humour embedded in the labour process was the central form of humour. Particularly important were forms of humour categorized as ‘routine humour’ and ‘routine absurdity’ in which workers played with the routine labour process. Overall, the humour in this factory primarily had resistive meanings, and contributed to an autonomous shopfloor culture which informed acts of informal collective resistance. At the same time, much of the humour lubricated the enactment of the labour process. The paper contributes to knowledge by drawing on Bergson to offer a theoretical framing for, and extending our understanding of, humour embedded in the labour process. Specifically, it suggests the importance of the dialectical sense of humour – humour which enacts the labour process, while having implicitly resistive meanings.

KeywordsHumor Studies and Applications; Dialectic; Sociology; Aesthetics; Emotional labor; Premise; Psychology; Social psychology; Epistemology; Art; Philosophy
Matched conceptsautonomous
Matched fieldsabstract

101. Meeting the Challenges of Intensive Parenting Culture: Gender, Risk Management and the Moral Parent

Sociology 2011-10-27 Available
JournalSociology
Publication date2011-10-27
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1177/0038038511416169
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Fiona ShiraniCardiff University; Cardiff University, UK
Karen HenwoodCardiff University; Cardiff University, UK
Carrie ColtartCardiff University; Cardiff University, UK
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

In recent years, academics have shown interest in the phenomenon of intensive parenting, which has predominantly focused on mothers as primary caregivers. In this article we seek to move beyond approaches which invoke a maternal lens in order to consider fathers’ experience of intensive parenting in relation to their lives with partners. Drawing on data from a qualitative longitudinal study of men over the transition to fatherhood we explore men’s experiences of the benefits and challenges of an intensive parenting approach. Despite increased involvement in childcare, men appear to be relatively insulated against the demands of intensive parenting, describing the importance of autonomous decision-making over following expert advice. However, this article considers the way in which other aspects of contemporary parenting may be experienced more intensively by men, pointing to gender differentiation in risks related to a moral parenthood identity.

KeywordsGender Roles and Identity Studies; Work-Family Balance Challenges; Youth Education and Societal Dynamics; Identity (music); Sociology; Phenomenon; Relation (database); Developmental psychology; Psychology; Qualitative research; Gender studies; Social psychology; Social science; Epistemology; Physics; Acoustics; Philosophy; Database; Computer science

Matched fieldsabstract

102. The impact of help seeking on individual task performance: The moderating effect of help seekers' logics of action.

Journal of Applied Psychology 2011-11-14 Available
JournalJournal of Applied Psychology
Publication date2011-11-14
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1037/a0026014
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Dvora GellerCollege of Management Academic Studies; School of Business Administration, College of Management, Rishon LeZion, Israel
Peter BambergerTel Aviv University; Recanati Graduate School of Business Administration, Tel Aviv University, Ramat Aviv, Israel
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Drawing from achievement-goal theory and the social psychological literature on help seeking, we propose that it is the variance in the logic underpinning employees' help seeking that explains divergent findings regarding the relationship between help seeking and task performance. Using a sample of 110 newly hired customer contact employees, a prospective study design, and archival performance data, we found no evidence of a hypothesized main effect of help seeking on performance. However, we did find that the help seeking-performance relationship was conditioned by the degree to which help seekers endorse 2 alternative help-seeking logics (autonomous vs. dependent logic) such that the level of help seeking is more strongly related to performance among those either more strongly endorsing an autonomous help-seeking logic or more weakly endorsing a dependent help-seeking logic.

KeywordsJob Satisfaction and Organizational Behavior; Gender Diversity and Inequality; Family Business Performance and Succession; Psychology; Seekers; Task (project management); Variance (accounting); Help-seeking; Social psychology; Action (physics); Sample (material); Mental health; Accounting; Chromatography; Psychotherapist; Physics; Management; Economics; Political science; Business; Chemistry; Law; Quantum mechanics

Matched fieldsabstract; title

103. Ironic Effects of Antiprejudice Messages

Psychological Science 2011-11-28 Available
JournalPsychological Science
Publication date2011-11-28
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1177/0956797611427918
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Lisa LegaultUniversity of Toronto; The Scarborough Hospital; University of Toronto Scarborough; University of Toronto , Scarborough, Ontario, Canada
Jennifer N. GutsellUniversity of Toronto; The Scarborough Hospital; University of Toronto Scarborough; University of Toronto Scarborough ON Canada
Michael InzlichtUniversity of Toronto; The Scarborough Hospital; University of Toronto Scarborough; University of Toronto Scarborough ON Canada
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Although prejudice-reduction policies and interventions abound, is it possible that some of them result in the precise opposite of their intended effect--an increase in prejudice? We examined this question by exploring the impact of motivation-based prejudice-reduction interventions and assessing whether certain popular practices might in fact increase prejudice. In two experiments, participants received detailed information on, or were primed with, the goal of prejudice reduction; the information and primes either encouraged autonomous motivation to regulate prejudice or emphasized the societal requirement to control prejudice. Ironically, motivating people to reduce prejudice by emphasizing external control produced more explicit and implicit prejudice than did not intervening at all. Conversely, participants in whom autonomous motivation to regulate prejudice was induced displayed less explicit and implicit prejudice compared with no-treatment control participants. We outline strategies for effectively reducing prejudice and discuss the detrimental consequences of enforcing antiprejudice standards.

KeywordsSocial and Intergroup Psychology; Cultural Differences and Values; Experimental Behavioral Economics Studies; Prejudice (legal term); Psychology; Social psychology; Psychological intervention; Control (management); Economics; Management; Psychiatry

Matched fieldsabstract

104. Why a Central Network Position Isn't Enough: The Role of Motivation and Ability for Knowledge Sharing in Employee Networks

Academy of Management Journal 2011-12-01 Available
JournalAcademy of Management Journal
Publication date2011-12-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2009.0007
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Mia ReinholtCopenhagen Business School; ScienceScope (United Kingdom); #N# 1Copenhagen Business School
Torben PedersenCopenhagen Business School; ScienceScope (United Kingdom); #N# 1Copenhagen Business School
Nicolai J. FossCopenhagen Business School; Norwegian School of Economics; Copenhagen Business School and Norwegian School of Economics and Business Administration; 2Copenhagen Business School and Norwegian School of Economics and Business Administration
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Contrasting views exist on how network characteristics predict knowledge sharing. Although large, open egocentric networks foster network positions that provide access to nonredundant knowledge, critics have noted that such networks impair knowledge sharing, because trust and reciprocity do not thrive in them. This problem may, however, be resolved by including motivation and ability to share knowledge as moderators of the association between network position and knowledge sharing. Our analysis of 705 employees in a consultancy shows that employees' knowledge acquisition and provision are highest when network centrality, autonomous motivation, and ability are all high, thus supporting the proposed three-way interaction.

KeywordsKnowledge Management and Sharing; Innovation and Knowledge Management; Social Capital and Networks; Position (finance); Employee motivation; Knowledge management; Knowledge sharing; Organizational behavior; Business; Psychology; Industrial organization; Marketing; Computer science; Social psychology; Finance
Matched conceptsautonomous
Matched fieldsabstract

105. Is Selection the Answer for Encouraging more Employee Autonomy?

Academy of Management Perspectives 2012-01-01 Available
JournalAcademy of Management Perspectives
Publication date2012-01-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.5465/amp.2012.0156
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Stuart D. Sidlenot available
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

When companies embrace new business models to become more innovative or customer focused they often need to inspire their employees to think differently about how they approach their jobs. And in some cases this can be extraordinarily diffi cult. Companies with long, established histories of providing strict oversight of even the smallest employee decisions, for example, will have a hard time implementing new initiatives that are intended to empower employees and allow them to make autonomous decisions. Indeed, employees are often slow to embrace new levels of autonomy even if doing so is in their own best interest. And although employee resistance to change is natural, if management does not deal with it effectively then new initiatives will fail. Consequently, executives committed to making change happen may want to implement new incentives and performance management programs to help overcome employee resistance. Unfortunately, certain types of employee behavior and decision-making processes are harder to infl uence with traditional management tools. For these situations, managers are faced with a dilemma: do they bring in new people who think and approach decisions in a way that is better aligned with new business strategies or do they apply management controls aimed at motivating resistant employees to change the way they approach decision making? Fortunately, new research by Dennis Campbell of Harvard University provides some insight to managers facing this dilemma. Campbell explored the role of selection in helping management ensure that employees embrace new strategic initiatives— especially those that require employees to approach decisions in new ways. Specifi cally, he studied whether different recruitment and selection tactics impact the likelihood that employees will engage in practices that are aligned with the organization’s new strategic objectives.

KeywordsExperimental Behavioral Economics Studies; Accounting and Organizational Management; Complex Systems and Decision Making; Autonomy; Selection (genetic algorithm); Employee engagement; Employee morale; Employee motivation; Business; Public relations; Knowledge management; Management; Marketing; Economics; Political science; Computer science; Engineering; Work (physics); Artificial intelligence; Mechanical engineering; Law
Matched conceptsautonomous
Matched fieldsabstract

106. Meta-Organizational Design: Rethinking Design in Inter-Organizational and Community Contexts

Digital Access to Scholarship at Harvard (DASH) (Harvard University) 2012-01-01 Available
JournalDigital Access to Scholarship at Harvard (DASH) (Harvard University)
Publication date2012-01-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1002/smj.1975/abstract
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Ranjay Gulatinot available
Phanish Puranamnot available
Michael L. Tushmannot available
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

This paper provides conceptual foundations for analyzing organizations comprising multiple legally autonomous entities, which we call meta-organizations. We assess the antecedents of the emergence of such collectives and the design choices they entail. The paper identifies key parameters on which such meta-organizations' designs differ from each other. It also presents a taxonomy that elucidates how such forms of collective action vary and the constraints they must address to be successful. We conclude with implications for research on meta-organizational design.

KeywordsOpen Source Software Innovations; Innovative Approaches in Technology and Social Development; Innovation and Knowledge Management; Organizational architecture; Business; Knowledge management; Process management; Sociology; Computer science
Matched conceptsautonomous
Matched fieldsabstract

107. Industry growth, work role characteristics, and job satisfaction: A cross-level mediation model.

Journal of Occupational Health Psychology 2012-01-01 Available
JournalJournal of Occupational Health Psychology
Publication date2012-01-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1037/a0029535
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Michael T. FordUniversity at Albany, State University of New York; Department of Psychology, University at Albany, SUNY, USA. [email removed]; Department of Psychology, University at Albany–SUNY, Albany, NY, US
Jessica D. WooldridgeUniversity at Albany, State University of New York; Department of Psychology, University at Albany–SUNY, Albany, NY, US
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

The associations between industry revenue growth, individual work role characteristics, and job satisfaction were examined in this cross-level mediation analysis. Work roles were expected to be more autonomous, involve greater skill variety, and offer more opportunities for growth and development for workers in growing industries than for workers in declining industries. Supervisor support was also hypothesized to be stronger for workers in high-growth industries. Results from a nationally representative (U.S.) sample of service industry workers, using multilevel modeling, supported these propositions and suggest that job enrichment mediates relations between industry growth and job satisfaction. Associations between industry growth and autonomy were also stronger among workers in occupations that are less normatively autonomous, suggesting that industry growth fosters a weakening, and industry decline a strengthening, of traditional differences in autonomy across work roles. These results contribute to a multilevel perspective on organizational environments, individual work roles, and worker attitudes and well-being.

KeywordsJob Satisfaction and Organizational Behavior; Workplace Health and Well-being; Employment and Welfare Studies; Job satisfaction; Autonomy; Mediation; Multilevel model; Supervisor; Job attitude; Psychology; Work (physics); Job design; Tertiary sector of the economy; Perspective (graphical); Variety (cybernetics); Job performance; Business; Social psychology; Marketing; Economics; Management; Political science; Engineering; Computer science; Mechanical engineering; Machine learning; Artificial intelligence; Law
Matched conceptsautonomous
Matched fieldsabstract

108. “I Help Because I Want to, Not Because You Tell Me to”

Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin 2012-02-09 Available
JournalPersonality and Social Psychology Bulletin
Publication date2012-02-09
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1177/0146167211435940
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Louisa PaveyKingston University; Kingston University, Kingston Upon Thames, UK
Tobias GreitemeyerUniversität Innsbruck; University of Innsbruck, Austria
Paul SparksUniversity of Sussex; University of Sussex, Brighton, UK
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Empathetic arousal has been found to be a strong predictor of helping behavior. However, research has neglected the motivational mechanisms whereby empathetic concern elicits help giving. Three studies examined the extent to which autonomous and controlled motives for helping mediated the relationship between empathy and helping. Study 1 found that state empathy predicted willingness to offer time and money to help a person in need, with this relationship mediated by autonomous motivation for helping. Study 2 demonstrated that dispositional, empathetic concern predicted prosocial intentions and behavior via the mediation of autonomous motivation. Study 3 revealed that participants who focused on the emotions of another person in distress reported greater willingness to help than did participants who remained emotionally detached, with this effect mediated by autonomous motivation to help. Controlled motivation had no positive effects on helping in any of the studies. The results suggest that empathy encourages prosocial behavior by increasing autonomous motivation to help.

KeywordsJob Satisfaction and Organizational Behavior; Attachment and Relationship Dynamics; Cultural Differences and Values; Prosocial behavior; Psychology; Empathy; Helping behavior; Social psychology; Personal distress; Mediation; Altruism (biology); Empathic concern; Arousal; Perspective-taking; Law; Political science

109. Ambidexterity and performance in multiunit contexts: Cross‐level moderating effects of structural and resource attributes

Strategic Management Journal 2012-03-12 Available
JournalStrategic Management Journal
Publication date2012-03-12
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1002/smj.1977
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Justin J.P. JansenErasmus University Rotterdam; Rotterdam School of Management, Erasmus University, Rotterdam, the Netherlands; Rotterdam School of Management Erasmus University; Rotterdam; The Netherlands
Zeki ŞimşekUniversity of Connecticut; School of Business, University of Connecticut, Storrs, Connecticut, U.S.A; School of Business, University Of Connecticut, Storrs, Connecticut, U.S.A
Qing CaoUniversity of Connecticut; School of Business, University of Connecticut, Storrs, Connecticut, U.S.A; School of Business, University Of Connecticut, Storrs, Connecticut, U.S.A
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Abstract Research suggests that unit‐level ambidexterity positively impacts subsequent unit performance but theory and testing on this impact remain impoverished. We develop a cross‐level model suggesting that structural and resource attributes of the organizational context significantly shape the relationship between unit ambidexterity and performance. Using multisource and lagged data from 285 organizational units located within 88 autonomous branches, results from hierarchical linear modeling show that this relationship is boosted when the organization is decentralized, more resource munificent, or less resource interdependent. We also find that structural differentiation of the organization does not condition the unit ambidexterity‐performance relationship. Through this cross‐level theory and testing, we develop a richer explanation of the effectiveness of ambidextrous units operating in multiunit contexts. Copyright © 2012 John Wiley &amp; Sons, Ltd.

Abstract This article provides conceptual foundations for analyzing organizations comprising multiple legally autonomous entities, which we call meta‐organizations. We assess the antecedents of the emergence of such collectives and the design choices they entail. The article identifies key parameters on which such meta‐organizations' designs differ from each other. 111. Microcredit in Palestine (1995–2008): A business history perspective

Business History 2012-06-01 Available
JournalBusiness History
Publication date2012-06-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/00076791.2011.638501
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Paolo Di MartinoBirmingham City University; South and City College Birmingham; University College Birmingham; University of Birmingham; Birmingham Business School, Birmingham, UK
Shaker SarsourUniversity of Siena; University of Siena, Siena, Italy
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

This article analyses the development and functioning of the microcredit industry in Palestine in the period between its establishment as a partially autonomous political entity (1994) and the Israeli invasion of the Gaza Strip (2008). The article shows how, despite the increase in potential demand for microcredit due to the deterioration of the economic environment, the growth of the sector has been below expectation. One of the most important causes of this phenomenon has been the reluctance to lend resulting from the growing risk of late or no repayment of loans. Using original data from one microcredit institution (Arab Centre for Agricultural Development) and a quantitative approach, the article investigates the causes of this problem. Results show that the risk of late repayment was negatively correlated to the level of interest rate, to macroeconomic conditions, and to the age of the borrower while it was positively associated to the share of investment in the Gaza Strip, and to the size of loans.

KeywordsMicrofinance and Financial Inclusion; Islamic Finance and Banking Studies; FinTech, Crowdfunding, Digital Finance; Business history; Palestine; Perspective (graphical); Business; Political science; Economics; Management; History; Ancient history; Computer science; Artificial intelligence

112. Autonomous work groups and the challenge of inter-group coordination

Human Relations 2012-06-01 Available
JournalHuman Relations
Publication date2012-06-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1177/0018726712448203
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Jonas A. IngvaldsenNorwegian University of Science and Technology; Norwegian University of Science and Technology, Trondheim, Norway,; Norwegian University of Science and Technology, Trondheim, Norway, [email removed]#N#
Monica RolfsenNorwegian University of Science and Technology; Norwegian University of Science and Technology, Trondheim, Norway,; Norwegian University of Science and Technology, Trondheim, Norway, [email removed]#N#
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

In work systems based on autonomous work groups, the effort of different autonomous groups needs to be coordinated to produce desirable system-wide effects. The existing literature on organizational structures for inter-group coordination is limited. In this article, the authors explore two different structures for inter-group coordination, which are alternatives to traditional hierarchical control. These structures are rotating group spokespersons and shared leadership. The main conclusion of this article is that inter-group coordination becomes a major challenge when groups enjoy high levels of autonomy. Our argument builds on a case study of a manufacturing company, analysed through the lens of modern socio-technical theory. The difficulties associated with inter-group coordination have implications for debates on organizational performance and may help explain why autonomous work groups are not widely used in industry.

KeywordsTeam Dynamics and Performance; Innovation and Knowledge Management; Construction Project Management and Performance; Autonomy; Group (periodic table); Work (physics); Argument (complex analysis); Working group; Knowledge management; Control (management); Group work; Organizational structure; Sociology; Psychology; Computer science; Engineering; Management; Political science; Artificial intelligence; Economics; Pedagogy; Mechanical engineering; Organic chemistry; Law; Chemistry; Biochemistry; Operating system

113. Does pre‐entry licensing undermine the performance of subsequent independent activities? Evidence from the global aerospace industry, 1944–2000

Strategic Management Journal 2012-06-28 Available
JournalStrategic Management Journal
Publication date2012-06-28
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1002/smj.2003
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Louis MulotteTilburg University; Tilburg University, Tilburg School of Economics and Management, Center for Innovation Research and CentER for Research in Economics and Business, Tilburg, the Netherlands
Pierre DussaugeHEC Paris; HEC Paris, Jouy-en-Josas, France
Will MitchellDuke University; University of Toronto; Duke University, The Fuqua School of Business, Durham, North Carolina, U.S.A. and The Rotman School of Management, University of Toronto, Toronto, Ontario, Canada
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Abstract We study how firms' use of in‐licensing for their initial entry to a business domain can detract from the performance of their subsequent autonomous endeavors in the domain. We argue that in‐licensing produces high levels of causal ambiguity about factors that drive the performance achieved with the licensed product. In turn, the experience that firms gather through pre‐entry licensing is likely to generate superstitious learning and overconfidence that undermine the performance of licensees' subsequent independent operations. The biases will be particularly strong in the face of contextual dissimilarity. We find consistent evidence in a study of firms that entered the global aircraft industry between 1944 and 2000. The research helps advance the understanding of the benefits and costs of markets for technology. Copyright © 2012 John Wiley &amp; Sons, Ltd.

KeywordsIntellectual Property and Patents; Innovation and Knowledge Management; Firm Innovation and Growth; Ambiguity; Overconfidence effect; Industrial organization; Aerospace; Marketing; Product (mathematics); Business; Domain (mathematical analysis); Economics; Computer science; Law; Psychology; Political science; Mathematics; Geometry; Social psychology; Programming language; Mathematical analysis
Matched conceptsautonomous
Matched fieldsabstract

114. The effects of work motivation on employee exhaustion and commitment: An extension of the JD-R model

Work & Stress 2012-07-01 Available
JournalWork & Stress
Publication date2012-07-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/02678373.2012.713202
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Claude FernetUniversité du Québec à Trois-Rivières; Department of Management, Université du Québec à Trois-Rivières, Canada
Stéphanie AustinUniversité du Québec à Trois-Rivières; Department of Management, Université du Québec à Trois-Rivières, Canada
Robert J. VallerandUniversité du Québec à Montréal; Department of Psychology, Université du Québec à Montréal, Canada; Department of psychology, Université du Québec à Montréal, Canada
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Abstract A model that integrates and builds on the job demands-resources model and self-determination theory is proposed to better understand the role of work motivation in relation to job resources, occupational commitment and emotional exhaustion. Two forms of motivation were studied: autonomous motivation, in which employees act with volition,; and controlled motivation, in which they act under internal or external pressure. Data were collected at two time points nine months apart from a sample of 586 school principals in Quebec, Canada. SEM analysis results support the hypothesized model. Specifically, job resources had a positive effect on autonomous motivation but a negative effect on controlled motivation. In addition, taking into account the cross-lagged effects of job resources on commitment and exhaustion, autonomous motivation had a negative effect on exhaustion but a positive effect on commitment whereas controlled motivation had a positive effect on exhaustion. These results advance the understanding of why work motivation acts on employee functioning and how it can play an active role in both the motivational and energetic processes of the job demands-resources model. Practical implications and further theoretical implications are discussed. Keywords: emotional exhaustionoccupational commitmentjob resourceswork motivationjob demands-resources modelself-determination theory Acknowledgements This work was supported by a grant from the Fonds de recherche du Québec – Société et culture and a fellowship from the Fonds de Recherche du Québec – Santé awarded to the first author.

KeywordsJob Satisfaction and Organizational Behavior; Motivation and Self-Concept in Sports; Workaholism, burnout, and well-being; Psychology; Emotional exhaustion; Volition (linguistics); Work motivation; Social psychology; Employee motivation; Need for achievement; Job performance; Work (physics); Burnout; Job satisfaction; Clinical psychology; Linguistics; Philosophy; Mechanical engineering; Engineering
Matched conceptsautonomous
Matched fieldsabstract

115. Distinguishing Autonomous and Directive Forms of Goal Support

Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin 2012-08-27 Available
JournalPersonality and Social Psychology Bulletin
Publication date2012-08-27
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1177/0146167212457075
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Richard KoestnerMcGill University; McGill University, Montreal, Quebec, Canada
Theodore A. PowersUniversity of Massachusetts Dartmouth; University of Massachusetts Dartmouth, USA; University of Massachusetts, Dartmouth, USA,
Noémie CarbonneauUniversité du Québec à Montréal; University of Quebec at Montreal, Canada; (University of Quebec at Montreal, Canada)
Marina MilyavskayaMcGill University; McGill University, Montreal, Quebec, Canada
Sook Ning ChuaMcGill University; McGill University, Montreal, Quebec, Canada
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Three studies examined the relations of autonomy support and directive support to goal progress over 3 months. Autonomy support was defined in terms of empathic perspective-taking, whereas directive support was defined in terms of the provision of positive guidance. Results from Study 1 revealed that autonomy support between romantic partners was significantly positively related to goal progress over 3 months, and that the beneficial effect of autonomy support was mediated by enhanced autonomous goal motivation. Study 2 involved female friend dyads and extended the goal progress results to include both self-reports and reports by peers. Study 3 showed that autonomy support similarly promoted progress at vicarious goals. Across three studies, autonomy support was also significantly associated with improved relationship quality and subjective well-being. Directive support was marginally associated with better goal progress across the three studies and unrelated to relationship quality or well-being.

KeywordsAttachment and Relationship Dynamics; Behavioral Health and Interventions; Child and Adolescent Psychosocial and Emotional Development; Directive; Autonomy; Psychology; Social psychology; Perspective (graphical); Quality (philosophy); Developmental psychology; Political science; Computer science; Philosophy; Epistemology; Artificial intelligence; Law; Programming language
Matched conceptsautonomous
Matched fieldstitle

116. ‘The Return of the Primal Father’ in Postmodernity? A Lacanian Analysis of Authentic Leadership

Organization Studies 2012-09-01 Available
JournalOrganization Studies
Publication date2012-09-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1177/0170840612448157
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Jana CostasFreie Universität Berlin; Freie Universität Berlin, Germany
Alireza TaheriAnna Freud Centre; Centre for Freudian Analysis and Research, London, UK; Centre for Freudian Analysis and Research , London, UK
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

A significant stream of research taking a psychoanalytic perspective has focused on leadership in organizations. Studies have investigated, from a Freudian perspective, leader–follower relations where the leader represents a domineering and authoritarian father figure. We contribute to this research by introducing Lacanian theory to explore the increasingly influential ‘authentic leadership’ approach. In contrast to traditional leadership, this approach advocates, in line with postmodern trends, a post-heroic, non-authoritarian and even self-effacing leader figure. In analysing academic and practitioner-oriented authentic leadership texts we ask, drawing on Lacanian insights, whether authentic leadership enables subjects to separate from the master discourse of traditional leadership through promoting something like the analyst discourse, or whether it entails the return of a phantasmagorical Freudian primal father figure, leading to heightened dependency. In so doing, we discuss the political significance of Lacanian theory in illuminating possibilities for more autonomous and emancipatory relations in organizations.

KeywordsManagement and Organizational Studies; Psychotherapy Techniques and Applications; Freudian slip; Sociology; Psychoanalytic theory; Perspective (graphical); Authoritarianism; Postmodernism; Authoritarian leadership style; Postmodernity; Epistemology; Leadership studies; Psychoanalysis; Politics; Psychology; Social psychology; Leadership style; Law; Philosophy; Political science; Democracy; Computer science; Artificial intelligence
Matched conceptsautonomous
Matched fieldsabstract

117. Constructing Rights and Wrongs in Humanitarian Action: Contributions from a Sociology of Praxis

Sociology 2012-10-01 Available
JournalSociology
Publication date2012-10-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1177/0038038512452357
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Dorothea HilhorstWageningen University & Research; Wageningen University, The Netherlands
Bram J. JansenWageningen University & Research; Wageningen University, The Netherlands
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Human rights entered the language and practice of humanitarian aid in the mid-1990s, and since then they have worked in parallel, complemented or competed with traditional frameworks ordering humanitarianism, including humanitarian principles, refugee law, and inter-agency standards. This article positions the study of rights within a sociology of praxis. It starts from a premise that interpretation and realisation of international norms depends on actors’ social negotiation. We seek to contribute to the sociology of rights with insights from legal pluralism and to analyse human rights as a semi-autonomous field in a multiplicity of normative frameworks. Based on cumulative research into humanitarian aid in disaster response, refugee care and protracted crises, the article explores how humanitarian agencies evoke different normative frameworks to legitimate their presence and programmes. How aid is shaped through the ‘rights speak’ of aid workers and recipients alike is illuminated by cases of programmes promoting women’s rights against sexual abuse from Kenya and the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC).

KeywordsMigration, Refugees, and Integration; Peacebuilding and International Security; Historical and Contemporary Political Dynamics; Human rights; Sociology; Premise; Praxis; Refugee; Humanitarian aid; Law; Dignity; Political science; Epistemology; Philosophy

Matched fieldsabstract

118. Self-determination, self-regulation, and the brain: Autonomy improves performance by enhancing neuroaffective responsiveness to self-regulation failure.

Journal of Personality and Social Psychology 2012-10-29 Available
JournalJournal of Personality and Social Psychology
Publication date2012-10-29
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1037/a0030426
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Lisa LegaultUniversity of Toronto; Psychology Department, University of Toronto, Toronto, ON, Canada
Michael InzlichtUniversity of Toronto; Psychology Department, University of Toronto, Toronto, ON, Canada
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

The importance of autonomous motivation in improving self-regulation has been a focal topic of motivation research for almost 3 decades. Despite this extensive research, however, there has not yet been a mechanistic account of how autonomous motivation works to boost self-regulatory functioning. To address this issue, we examined the role of autonomy in 2 basic self-regulation tasks while recording a neural signal of self-regulation failure (i.e., the error-related negativity; ERN). Based on the notion that autonomy improves self-regulation, we anticipated that autonomous motivation would enhance neuroaffective responsiveness to self-regulatory failure and thus improve performance relative to controlled motivation. In Study 1 (N = 43), we found that trait autonomy was positively associated with self-regulatory performance and that this effect was mediated by increased brain-based sensitivity to self-regulation failure, as demonstrated by a larger ERN. Study 2 (N = 55) replicated and extended this pattern using an experimental manipulation of autonomy; when autonomous motivation was contextually supported, task performance increased relative to those for whom autonomy was undermined and those in a neutral condition. In addition, this effect was mediated by both increased perceptions of autonomy and larger ERN amplitudes. These findings offer deeper insight into the links among motivational orientation, brain-based performance monitoring, and self-regulation.

KeywordsMotivation and Self-Concept in Sports; Behavioral Health and Interventions; Neural and Behavioral Psychology Studies; Autonomy; Psychology; Trait; Social psychology; Negativity effect; Task (project management); Perception; Brain activity and meditation; Regulatory focus theory; Self-determination theory; Cognitive psychology; Developmental psychology; Electroencephalography; Neuroscience; Political science; Computer science; Creativity; Economics; Management; Programming language; Law
Matched conceptsautonomous
Matched fieldsabstract

119. Resistance in Unjust Times: Archer, Structured Agency and the Sociology of Health Inequalities

Sociology 2012-12-17 Available
JournalSociology
Publication date2012-12-17
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1177/0038038512455878
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Graham ScamblerMortimer Market Centre; University College London; University College London, UK; Academic Department of Infection and Population Health, UCL, Mortimer Market Centre, London WC1E 6JB, UK
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

. Its association with a rapid increase of social and material inequality is beyond dispute. This article opens with a brief summation of contemporary British trends in economic inequalities, and finds an echo of these trends in health inequalities. It is suggested that the sociology of health inequalities in Britain lacks an analysis of agency, and that such an analysis is crucial. A case is made that the recent critical realist contribution of Margaret Archer on 'internal conversations' lends itself to an understanding of agency that is salient here. The article develops her typology of internal conversations to present characterizations of the 'focused autonomous reflexives' whose mind-sets are causally efficacious for producing and reproducing inequalities, and the 'dedicated meta-reflexives' whose casts of mind might yet predispose them to mobilize resistance to inequalities.

KeywordsCritical Realism in Sociology; Social and Cultural Dynamics; Contemporary Sociological Theory and Practice; Sociology; Agency (philosophy); Inequality; Resistance (ecology); Structure and agency; Medical sociology; Social science; Epistemology; Public health; Philosophy; Medicine; Nursing; Mathematical analysis; Ecology; Biology; Mathematics

Matched fieldsabstract

120. Coordinating Futures: Toward a Theory of Anticipation

American Journal of Sociology 2013-01-01 Available
JournalAmerican Journal of Sociology
Publication date2013-01-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1086/668646
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Iddo TavoryNew School; New School for Social Research
Nina EliasophSouthern California University for Professional Studies; University of Southern California
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

This article presents a theoretical approach for studying the coordination of futures. Building off theories of temporality and action, the authors map three different modes of future making—protentions, trajectories, and temporal landscapes—that actors need to coordinate in order to make sense of action together. Using a wide range of empirical evidence, they then show that these modes of future-coordination are autonomous from each other, so that although they are connected, they can clash or move in disjointed directions in interaction. By focusing on the coordination and disjunctures of those three modes, the authors argue that sociologists can provide a methodological axis of comparison between cases; depict mechanisms through which other theoretical or empirical constructs—such as racism or late modernity—operate; and open a window into the ways in which people organize and coordinate their futures, a topic of inquiry in its own right.

KeywordsSocial and Cultural Dynamics; Contemporary Sociological Theory and Practice; Critical Realism in Sociology; Temporality; Futures contract; Anticipation (artificial intelligence); Action (physics); Epistemology; Sociology; TRACE (psycholinguistics); Futures studies; Order (exchange); Modernity; Perspective (graphical); Computer science; Economics; Artificial intelligence; Philosophy; Quantum mechanics; Physics; Financial economics; Linguistics; Finance

Matched fieldsabstract

121. The Role of University in Regional Development – Case Study

Management Science 2013-01-01 Available
JournalManagement Science
Publication date2013-01-01
DOI URLmissing
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Maja Lamza Maronićnot available
Zdenka Barišićnot available
Jerko Glavašnot available
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Universities play a very important role in regional development as they contribute to regional economies and regional development. It is a generally accepted fact around the world that human capital is a major tool for achieving competitive advantages in the global market. An efficient society, which builds its competitive advantages on knowledge, must have a flexible labour market, adequate levels of employee security and protection, but also a system that continuously fosters and rewards investment into knowledge. People, their needs, motivation and satisfaction have therefore come into the focus of human resource management. An autonomous university, which is willing to adapt to changes and fulfil the requirements of the community, is a driving force behind overall development. The aim of this paper approach is to improve interactive communication, and to make broader use of university resources in the process of higher education.

KeywordsHigher Education Governance and Development; Educational Innovations and Challenges; Business; Process (computing); Investment (military); Human capital; Human resources; Competitive advantage; Marketing; Knowledge management; Industrial organization; Economics; Economic growth; Management; Computer science; Political science; Law; Operating system; Politics

122. Synthesizing and Extending Resource Dependence Theory

Journal of Management 2013-02-01 Available
JournalJournal of Management
Publication date2013-02-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1177/0149206312471391
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Johannes M. DreesVrije Universiteit Amsterdam; VU University Amsterdam
Pursey HeugensErasmus University Rotterdam
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Resource dependence theory (RDT) has long been a premier framework for understanding organization-environmental relations, but an empirical synthesis of its predictions is still lacking. Using meta-analysis, we consolidate 157 tests of RDT and corroborate its main predictions: organizations respond to resource dependencies by forming interorganizational arrangements like interlocks, alliances, joint ventures, in-sourcing arrangements, and mergers and acquisitions. In turn, these arrangements make them more autonomous and more legitimate. We also extend RDT in three ways. First, we “unpack” the theory by showing that the mechanisms linking arrangement formation to organizational autonomy and legitimacy differ across arrangements. Second, we address the question whether RDT is also a theory of organizational performance. We find that whereas autonomy positively mediates the relationship between arrangement formation and performance, legitimacy does not. This suggests that RDT can also explain organizational actions that have societal acceptance rather than economic performance as an ulterior motive. Third, we assess whether competition law is a boundary condition to RDT’s prescriptions. Specifically, we show that the adoption of the Horizontal Merger Guidelines in the U.S. has caused organizations to “flee” from mergers to less regulated arrangements like alliances and joint ventures, and has hurt the profitability of the remaining mergers.

KeywordsInnovation and Knowledge Management; Corporate Finance and Governance; Business Strategy and Innovation; Resource dependence theory; Business; Legitimacy; Industrial organization; Profitability index; Autonomy; Resource (disambiguation); Competition (biology); Resource-based view; Mergers and acquisitions; Transaction cost; Closeness; Competitive advantage; Marketing; Microeconomics; Economics; Political science; Biology; Politics; Computer network; Computer science; Mathematics; Ecology; Law; Finance; Mathematical analysis
Matched conceptsautonomous
Matched fieldsabstract

123. Changing job roles in the Norwegian and UK fitness industry: in search of national institutional effects

Work Employment and Society 2013-02-01 Available
JournalWork Employment and Society
Publication date2013-02-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1177/0950017012460325
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Caroline LloydCardiff University; Cardiff University, UK
Jonathan PayneDe Montfort University; De Montfort University, UK
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

There has been considerable debate about the impact of different national institutional environments on work organization. The Nordic countries, with their strong trade unions and well developed systems of social partnership around collective bargaining and vocational education and training, are found to be particularly advanced when it comes to developing more autonomous job roles. While institutions are said to play a key role, some commentators point to the existence of national employment ‘logics’ which may have a more far-reaching influence. Drawing upon qualitative research, the article compares the job of fitness instructor in Norway and the UK. The study finds little evidence of a clear country-level difference in job design, suggesting that if there is a national employment ‘logic’ it has been overwhelmed by specific industry dynamics.

KeywordsLabor Movements and Unions; Social Policy and Reform Studies; Cultural Industries and Urban Development; Norwegian; General partnership; Vocational education; Social Partnership; Collective bargaining; Public relations; Work (physics); Political science; Social partners; Labour economics; Economic growth; Economics; Public administration; Linguistics; Engineering; Philosophy; Mechanical engineering; Law

124. The Autonomy Paradox: The Implications of Mobile Email Devices for Knowledge Professionals

Organization Science 2013-02-13 Available
JournalOrganization Science
Publication date2013-02-13
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1287/orsc.1120.0806
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Melissa MazmanianUniversity of California, Irvine; Department of Informatics, School of Information and Computer Sciences, University of California, Irvine, Irvine, California 92697
Wanda J. OrlikowskiMIT Sloan School of Management, Cambridge, Massachusetts 02142
JoAnne YatesMIT Sloan School of Management, Cambridge, Massachusetts 02142
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Our research examines how knowledge professionals use mobile email devices to get their work done and the implications of such use for their autonomy to control the location, timing, and performance of work. We found that knowledge professionals using mobile email devices to manage their communication were enacting a norm of continual connectivity and accessibility that produced a number of contradictory outcomes. Although individual use of mobile email devices offered these professionals flexibility, peace of mind, and control over interactions in the short term, it also intensified collective expectations of their availability, escalating their engagement and thus reducing their ability to disconnect from work. Choosing to use their mobile email devices to work anywhere/anytime—actions they framed as evidence of their personal autonomy—the professionals were ending up using it everywhere/all the time, thus diminishing their autonomy in practice. This autonomy paradox reflected professionals’ ongoing navigation of the tension between their interests in personal autonomy on the one hand and their professional commitment to colleagues and clients on the other. We further found that this dynamic has important unintended consequences—reaffirming and challenging workers’ sense of themselves as autonomous and responsible professionals while also collectively shifting the norms of how work is and should be performed in the contemporary workplace.

KeywordsPersonal Information Management and User Behavior; Knowledge Management and Sharing; Team Dynamics and Performance; Autonomy; Flexibility (engineering); Public relations; Mobile device; Norm (philosophy); Work (physics); Control (management); Internet privacy; Psychology; Knowledge management; Business; Social psychology; Computer science; Political science; Management; World Wide Web; Engineering; Artificial intelligence; Mechanical engineering; Economics; Law

Matched fieldsabstract

125. Blending personal and managerial capitalism: Bertelsmann's rise from medium-sized publisher to global media corporation and service provider, 1950–2010

Business History 2013-03-01 Available
JournalBusiness History
Publication date2013-03-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/00076791.2012.744584
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Hartmut BerghoffDeutsches Historisches Institut Washington; German Historical Institute, Washington DC / University of Göttingen
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

This article suggests that the distinction between family and managerial capitalism is not as clear as often assumed and presents empirical evidence for hybrid forms of governance. It is based on a case study of Europe's largest media company, Bertelsmann AG, which grew from a mid-sized firm into a large multinational after 1950. Family-led until 1981, Bertelsmann is still family-controlled today. The article analyses the structure of the family, the remodelling of ownership and controlling rights, the methods of financing, and the role of non-family managers. On all levels, creative solutions were found that overcame traditional forms of patriarchal family capitalism and introduced strong elements of managerial capitalism into a family business. Managers enjoyed considerable space for autonomous action providing they did not challenge the ground rules set by the family.

KeywordsFamily Business Performance and Succession; Corporate Finance and Governance; Capitalism; Corporation; Corporate governance; Multinational corporation; Family business; Business; Space (punctuation); Service (business); Accounting; Sociology; Economics; Finance; Law; Marketing; Political science; Politics; Linguistics; Philosophy

126. Lunch Breaks Unpacked: The Role of Autonomy as a Moderator of Recovery during Lunch

Academy of Management Journal 2013-03-26 Available
JournalAcademy of Management Journal
Publication date2013-03-26
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2011.1072
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
John P. TrougakosUniversity of Toronto; University of TorontoWilfrid Laurier UniversityHong Kong Polytechnic UniversityUniversity of Texas-San Antonio
Ivona HidegWilfrid Laurier University; University of TorontoWilfrid Laurier UniversityHong Kong Polytechnic UniversityUniversity of Texas-San Antonio
Bonnie Hayden ChengHong Kong Polytechnic University; University of TorontoWilfrid Laurier UniversityHong Kong Polytechnic UniversityUniversity of Texas-San Antonio
Daniel J. BealThe University of Texas at San Antonio; University of Texas-San Antonio; University of TorontoWilfrid Laurier UniversityHong Kong Polytechnic UniversityUniversity of Texas-San Antonio
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Work recovery research has focused mainly on how after-work break activities help employees replenish their resources and reduce fatigue. Given that employees spend a considerable amount of time at work, understanding how they can replenish their resources during the workday is critical. Drawing on ego depletion (Muraven & Baumeister, 2000) and self-determination theory (Deci & Ryan, 1985), we employed multi-source experience sampling methods to test the effects of a critical boundary condition, employee lunch break autonomy, on the relation between lunch break activities and end-of-workday fatigue. Although specific energy-relevant activities had a main effect on end-of-workday fatigue, each of these was moderated by the degree of autonomous choice associated with the break. Specifically, for activities that supported the psychological needs of relatedness and competence (i.e., social and work activities, respectively), as lunch break autonomy increased, effects switched from increasing fatigue to reducing fatigue. To the extent that lunch break activities involved relaxation, however, lunch break autonomy was only important when levels of relaxation were low. We conclude that lunch break autonomy plays a complex and pivotal role in conferring the potential energetic benefits of lunch break activities. Contributions to theory and practice are discussed.

KeywordsJob Satisfaction and Organizational Behavior; Behavioral Health and Interventions; Psychological Well-being and Life Satisfaction; Deci-; Autonomy; Moderation; Psychology; Social psychology; Competence (human resources); Law; Political science
Matched conceptsautonomous
Matched fieldsabstract

127. Direct participation and the quality of work

Human Relations 2013-04-01 Available
JournalHuman Relations
Publication date2013-04-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1177/0018726712473035
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Duncan GallieUniversity of Oxford; University of Oxford, UK,; University of Oxford, UK, [email removed]#N#
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

The article compares the importance for the quality of work of three forms of direct participation – individual task discretion, semi-autonomous teamwork and consultative participation – drawing on a representative national survey of British employees. It assesses their implications for employee welfare, specifically their subjective importance, their implications for learning new skills and their effects on psychological well-being. It finds that individual task discretion is the most effective form of direct participation, followed by consultative participation. Although there are also positive effects of semi-autonomous teamwork, these are weaker and less consistently significant. The article finds that the strength of the implications of direct participation vary between employees, particularly by occupational class and the importance employees attach to the use of initiative at work. However, with the exception of those with weak initiative orientation, its effects are generally positive across different categories of employee.

KeywordsLabor Movements and Unions; Work-Family Balance Challenges; Gender Diversity and Inequality; Discretion; Teamwork; Task (project management); Work (physics); Welfare; Quality (philosophy); Psychology; Public relations; Social psychology; Political science; Management; Economics; Engineering; Epistemology; Mechanical engineering; Law; Philosophy

128. How Organizations Foster the Creative Use of Resources

Academy of Management Journal 2013-05-15 Available
JournalAcademy of Management Journal
Publication date2013-05-15
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2012.0048
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Scott SonensheinRice University; **Rice University;
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Using a multi-year qualitative study, I explain how employees at a fast-growing retail organization used creative resourcing—that is, the manipulation and recombination of objects in novel and useful ways to solve problems. I induce two core organizational processes (autonomous resourcing and directed resourcing) that explain how organizations foster ongoing creative activities in response to different perceived resource endowments. In doing so, I add clarity to a mixed literature that argues on the one hand that limited resources foster creativity, and on the other, that abundant resources foster creativity. Instead, I reorient the questions that scholars ask by shifting the conversation away from variance-explanation models and towards understanding organizational processes, specifically around how employees use resources in dynamic ways and how managers enable them to do so. My study unpacks how the link between resources and creativity is rooted deeply in the actions of managers and employees embedded in organizations over time. In elaborating theory around these actions, I contribute to scholarly and practitioner understanding around how organizations foster creativity in a variety of resource environments.

KeywordsCreativity in Education and Neuroscience; Management and Organizational Studies; Accounting and Organizational Management; Creativity; CLARITY; Conversation; Variety (cybernetics); Public relations; Resource (disambiguation); Sociology; Organizational theory; Knowledge management; Business; Psychology; Management; Social psychology; Political science; Computer science; Economics; Artificial intelligence; Chemistry; Communication; Computer network; Biochemistry
